article.headline: Kalman Filter — From Theory to OnStep Commands
article.deck: A telescope mount drifts. The Kalman filter predicts how much. The plate solver measures how far off. Then HYBRID sends a correction pulse. Here is every step, from matrix algebra to motor command.

sidebar.stat_desc: The gain matrix maps 2D measurement errors to 4D state corrections — the dimensional bridge between observation and action.

body.h2_6: 6. The Matrix Bug That Broke Everything
body.p_1: Every telescope mount drifts. No motor is perfectly smooth, no gear is perfectly round, no pier is perfectly vertical. The result is that long-exposure images trail, stars elongate, and sub-arcsecond resolution becomes impossible. For a century, astronomers accepted this as a fact of life — buy better gears, spend hours on polar alignment, and hope for the best. HYBRID Telescope Control takes a different approach: predict the drift mathematically, measure the residual error, and correct it in real time. At the center of this system sits the Kalman filter, a 60-year-old algorithm that turns noisy, incomplete measurements into a precise estimate of where your telescope is pointing — and where it will be five seconds from now.
body.p_2: Telescope tracking errors come from everywhere. The worm gear in an equatorial mount introduces periodic error — a sinusoidal wobble that repeats once per worm revolution, typically producing 30 to 120 arcseconds of peak-to-peak deviation. Atmospheric refraction bends starlight, and the amount of bending depends on the star's altitude above the horizon. A star at 30° elevation is refracted 1.7 arcminutes upward; at 10°, the refraction exceeds 5 arcminutes. Temperature changes flex the telescope tube, shifting the focal plane. Wind gusts push the optical tube assembly like a sail. Polar alignment error introduces field rotation that compounds over hours. And the sidereal rate itself — the constant motor speed that tracks the stars — is an open-loop command. It tells the mount where the stars should be, but never checks whether the mount is actually there.
body.p_3: The result is a system with no feedback. The motor runs, the stars drift, and the only way to know if the tracking is working is to take a picture and look at the stars. HYBRID changes this by closing the loop: it plate-solves an image every five seconds, measures the exact pointing error, and sends a correction to the mount before the error can accumulate into visible trailing.
body.p_4: The Kalman filter is a recursive estimator. Unlike a batch method that stores all previous measurements and reprocesses them, the Kalman filter keeps only the current state estimate and its uncertainty. Every new measurement updates the estimate, and the old measurements are discarded. This makes it extraordinarily efficient — the computational cost per step is constant, regardless of how long the system has been running.
body.p_5: In HYBRID, the state vector has four components:
body.p_6: The position terms capture the current pointing error in arcseconds along the right ascension and declination axes. The velocity terms capture the rate of change — how fast the error is growing or shrinking. Together, these four numbers describe everything the filter needs to know about the current tracking state.
body.p_7: This adaptive weighting is what makes the Kalman filter superior to a simple proportional controller. A proportional controller always applies the same correction for the same error. The Kalman filter considers the history of errors, the expected noise, and the system dynamics to produce corrections that are not just reactive but predictive.
body.p_8: Every five seconds, HYBRID executes a precise sequence of operations. Each step transforms data from one representation to the next, building toward a correction command that the mount can execute.
body.p_9: The plate-solve step is the only point where the real sky enters the system. Everything else is mathematics. The plate solver identifies stars in a short exposure, matches them against a catalog, and reports how far the telescope is from its intended pointing position. This raw pixel error is then converted to arcseconds using the plate scale — the angular size of each pixel, determined by the telescope's focal length and the camera's pixel size.
body.p_10: The filter outputs a four-element state vector in arcseconds. To convert this into a command the mount can execute, HYBRID must translate arcsecond errors into millisecond pulse durations. The ASCOM PulseGuide interface accepts a direction (North, South, East, West) and a duration in milliseconds. The mount firmware interprets these pulses as motor steps at the configured guide rate.
body.p_12: Here, 15.041 arcseconds per second is the sidereal rate — the speed at which the Earth rotates, and therefore the speed at which a perfectly aligned equatorial mount must track to keep a star centered. The division by 3600 converts arcseconds to degrees, and the guide rate factor (typically 0.5× sidereal) determines how fast the correction pulse moves the mount. The result is the number of milliseconds the motor must run at guide speed to close the measured error.
body.p_13: The velocity terms in the state vector provide an additional advantage: they predict future error. If the RA error is currently 2 arcseconds and growing at 0.4 arcseconds per second, the filter knows that by the time the next plate solve arrives (5 seconds later), the error will be approximately 4 arcseconds. The correction command accounts for this predicted growth, applying a slightly larger pulse than the current error alone would require. This predictive component is what separates Kalman-based tracking from simple proportional-integral-derivative (PID) controllers, which can only react to the current error and must always lag behind a changing signal.
body.p_14: The standard Kalman filter assumes a linear system — the state evolves according to a linear equation, and the measurement is a linear function of the state. For a well-aligned equatorial mount, this assumption holds reasonably well. The tracking error grows approximately linearly over short intervals, and the plate-solve measurement is a direct linear readout of position.
body.p_15: HYBRID runs both filters simultaneously. The standard Kalman filter handles equatorial mounts, where the linear assumption is adequate. The EKF handles alt-azimuth mounts, where field rotation and refraction create genuine nonlinear dynamics. The fallback to EKF was added after field testing revealed that the standard filter could not converge on alt-az mounts at low elevation angles, where refraction gradients are steepest.
body.p_16: The result was an index overflow: the code attempted to write a two-element result into a four-element array, corrupting memory and producing nonsensical state updates. The filter appeared to work when the error was small (because the corruption happened at the end of the array), but diverged wildly when the error was large (because the corrupted values dominated the next prediction step).
body.p_17: The numbers tell the story. Before the Kalman filter, HYBRID's tracking accuracy was limited by the mount's mechanical periodic error: typically 5 to 15 arcseconds RMS, depending on the gear quality and polar alignment. After introducing the Kalman filter, the RMS error dropped to 0.5 to 2 arcseconds — a tenfold improvement that brings long-exposure imaging within reach of modest equipment.
body.p_18: The filter adapts in real time. A wind gust that would have trailed a 30-second exposure is caught within the five-second plate-solve cycle, corrected by a millisecond-scale pulse, and never appears in the final image. A temperature-induced focal shift that would have defocused the stars is predicted by the velocity terms and compensated before it becomes visible. The mount does not become mechanically better. It becomes computationally aware — and that makes all the difference.
body.p_19: Each correction cycle has two phases. First, the filter predicts: it uses the velocity terms to extrapolate the state forward in time. If the RA error was growing at 0.3 arcseconds per second during the last cycle, the filter predicts it will be 0.3 arcseconds larger in the next cycle. Second, the filter updates: it takes the plate-solve measurement, compares it to the prediction, and blends them together using the Kalman gain. The gain is a weighting factor that determines how much to trust the prediction versus the measurement. If the measurement is noisy — perhaps because thin clouds scattered the starlight — the gain decreases, and the filter leans more on its prediction. If the prediction is wrong — perhaps because a wind gust suddenly pushed the mount — the gain increases, and the filter trusts the measurement more.
body.p_20: The Kalman predict step uses a simple constant-velocity model. Between plate solves, the filter assumes the error will continue to change at the same rate. The predict step also grows the uncertainty covariance P, reflecting the fact that we become less certain about the state as time passes without a measurement. The update step then shrinks that uncertainty by incorporating the new measurement. The Kalman gain K is computed fresh at each step, adapting to the current noise levels and prediction uncertainty.
body.p_21: Telescope tracking, however, contains nonlinear terms that the standard filter cannot capture. Atmospheric refraction follows a sec(z) law, where z is the zenith angle. The refraction at 80° altitude is not simply twice the refraction at 60° — the relationship is nonlinear. Alt-azimuth mounts introduce field rotation, where the angle between the camera frame and the sky changes continuously as the mount tracks. Mount geometry errors — flexure, orthogonality, tube sag — produce corrections that depend on the current pointing direction in nonlinear ways.
body.p_22: The Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) handles these nonlinearities by linearizing the system equations at each step using a Jacobian matrix. Instead of using a fixed state transition matrix F, the EKF computes F as the partial derivative of the state transition function with respect to the state, evaluated at the current estimate. This produces a locally linear approximation that captures the first-order nonlinear effects.
body.p_23: In the early development of HYBRID's Kalman filter, a single matrix dimension error caused weeks of erratic behavior. The Kalman gain matrix K has dimensions n × m, where n is the number of states (4) and m is the number of measurements (2: RA and Dec). The transpose of K is therefore m × n, or 2 × 4. When the code multiplied the transposed gain by the two-element innovation vector, the result was a two-element vector. But the state update required a four-element vector — one correction for each state component.
body.p_24: The fix was straightforward once identified: the innovation must be computed as a two-element vector (the difference between the two-element measurement and the two-element predicted measurement), then multiplied by the 4×2 gain matrix to produce a four-element state correction. The dimensions must flow consistently: K (4×2) × innovation (2×1) = correction (4×1). Every matrix operation in the filter was audited for dimension consistency, and a validation layer was added that checks dimensions at runtime in debug builds.
body.p_25: With the machine-learning enhancement that dynamically tunes the filter's noise parameters, sub-arcsecond tracking becomes possible in good conditions. The ML model observes the relationship between predicted and measured errors over time, adjusting the process noise covariance Q and measurement noise covariance R to match the current atmospheric and mechanical conditions. On a steady night with good seeing, the filter converges to extremely tight bounds. On a turbulent night with clouds, it gracefully degrades, leaning more heavily on its predictions and applying smaller, more conservative corrections.

article.headline: The ML + Kalman Hybrid Architecture
article.deck: The Kalman filter reacts to drift. The ML layer predicts it. Together they form a hybrid system that learns the telescope's personality — adapting to wind, temperature, periodic error, and mechanical wear in real time.

sidebar.stat_desc: Position, temporal, environmental, and statistical features extracted from each plate-solve cycle feed the prediction model.

body.h2_1: Why ML on Top of Kalman?
body.h2_6: Results: From Dead Code to Sub-Arcsecond
body.p_1: Every telescope has a personality. Some mounts exhibit periodic error that repeats like clockwork every 480 seconds. Others drift with the wind, their steel tubes expanding under temperature gradients that shift the optical axis by fractions of an arcsecond. The community has long relied on autoguiding — a reactive loop that measures a star's position and corrects after the fact. But the Extended Kalman Filter, when augmented with a machine learning layer, does something far more elegant: it learns to anticipate these errors before they manifest, and adjusts its own internal parameters to neutralize them.
body.p_2: The Extended Kalman Filter is, at its core, a reactive system. It observes a measurement, compares it to its prediction, computes an innovation, and then adjusts its state estimate based on the ratio of predicted uncertainty to observed uncertainty. This works beautifully for random, uncorrelated noise. But telescope drift is not random. Periodic error is deterministic. Wind gusts arrive in patterns. Temperature curves follow predictable arcs through the night. A purely reactive filter cannot exploit this structure — it can only respond to what it has already seen.
body.p_3: Machine learning changes the equation. An ML model trained on historical tracking data can identify correlations between environmental conditions, mount position, and observed drift. It can learn that at hour angle +2.5, with humidity at 65% and temperature dropping, this particular mount tends to develop a Dec drift of 0.3 arcseconds per minute. The Kalman filter would only discover this drift after it had already introduced error into the image. The ML layer predicts it in advance and feeds that prediction back into the filter's tuning parameters.
body.p_4: The hybrid approach assigns each layer what it does best. The Kalman filter handles immediate, high-frequency corrections — the fast loop that keeps stars round during a 120-second exposure. The ML layer operates on a slower cycle, analyzing accumulated data from multiple plate-solve cycles to tune the Kalman filter's process noise and measurement noise parameters. When the ML model detects that systematic drift is accelerating (perhaps due to a periodic error cycle entering its worst phase), it increases process noise scaling, telling the filter to trust measurements more aggressively. When observations become noisy (clouds passing, poor seeing degrading star profiles), it increases measurement noise scaling, telling the filter to rely more on its internal prediction model.
body.p_5: This creates a self-tuning filter that adapts to changing conditions in real time. The Kalman filter never knows the ML layer exists — it simply receives adjusted noise parameters and responds accordingly. The ML layer never directly modifies the state estimate — it only adjusts the knobs that control how the filter weighs competing information. This clean separation means the system degrades gracefully: if the ML model's quality drops below the confidence threshold, the filter continues operating with its baseline parameters, exactly as it would without any ML augmentation.
body.p_6: The hybrid system operates as a continuous four-step pipeline: collect, train, predict, inject. Each step has clear inputs, outputs, and timing constraints.
body.p_7: Every plate-solve cycle — typically every 5 to 30 seconds during imaging — records a feature vector. This is not a snapshot of a single measurement; it is a structured record containing the current RA and Dec errors in arcseconds, the timestamp, the hour angle and altitude of the telescope, the azimuth, and environmental data pulled from the weather station: temperature in degrees Celsius, humidity as a percentage, wind speed in meters per second, and barometric pressure in hectopascals. Alongside these raw values, the system records derived features: the RA and Dec rates of change, the filter's own prediction versus the actual observed error, and statistical aggregates from the last N cycles including error standard deviation, drift trend from a linear regression fit, innovation magnitude, residual norm, and a convergence indicator flag. The session duration and plate solve count round out the vector. In total, this produces 24 features per cycle — a compact but information-rich representation of the telescope's current state.
body.p_8: When the session starts, or when the user triggers a manual retrain, the system feeds accumulated feature vectors into a Stochastic Dual Coordinate Ascent (SDCA) regression engine. Two separate models are trained: one for RA drift and one for Dec drift. Each model receives the same 24-feature input vector but learns different weight coefficients, because the physical causes of RA and Dec error are fundamentally different — RA error is driven primarily by periodic error in the worm gear and atmospheric refraction modeling, while Dec error is driven by polar alignment error, flexure, and wind loading. The training labels are the actual observed drift values from subsequent plate-solve cycles. After fitting, the model reports an R² score — a measure of how much variance in the drift data the model explains. An R² of 1.0 means perfect prediction; 0.0 means the model is no better than a horizontal line. The R² score becomes the confidence gate for the entire system.
body.p_9: During imaging, the trained ML model runs on every plate-solve cycle. It receives the current 24-feature vector and outputs the expected RA drift and expected Dec drift for the current conditions. Alongside the drift predictions, it provides the confidence score (the R² value), and crucially, the derived noise scale factors: the process noise scale and measurement noise scale. These scale factors are what the Kalman filter will actually consume. The ML model does not directly inject a correction into the state estimate — it adjusts the filter's tuning parameters, leaving the filter to perform its own optimal estimation with the adjusted weights.
body.p_10: The injection step is where the ML layer meets the Kalman filter. The system checks the R² confidence score. If R² > 0.5, the model is deemed useful and ML tuning is engaged. The process noise covariance matrix is scaled by the ML-derived process noise scale factor, and the measurement noise covariance is scaled by the measurement noise scale factor. If R² < 0.5, the system falls back to the raw Kalman filter with baseline noise parameters — no ML augmentation. This gate ensures that a poorly trained model cannot degrade tracking quality. The model must prove it understands the telescope's behavior before it is allowed to influence the filter.
body.p_11: The feature vector is the lingua franca between the data collection pipeline and the ML engine. Every element serves a specific physical or statistical purpose.
body.p_12: The first group captures the telescope's current pointing state and recent error history. RA error and Dec error in arcseconds provide the raw measurement of how far off the current position is from the target. RA rate and Dec rate in arcseconds per second describe how fast the error is changing — a critical input for predicting where the error will be in the next cycle. Hour angle tells the ML model where the target is in the sky relative to the meridian, which directly affects periodic error phase and atmospheric refraction. Altitude and azimuth provide the geometric context for wind loading and gravitational flexure models.
body.p_13: The timestamp in Unix epoch format provides the absolute time reference. UT hour (0 to 24) and LST hour (0 to 24) capture the sidereal time context, which correlates with periodic error phase and the rate at which the mount's gear train rotates. The Julian date fraction provides a continuous time reference independent of calendar boundaries, useful for detecting multi-night trends.
body.p_14: Temperature in degrees Celsius is the single most important environmental feature. Thermal expansion of the mount, tube, and pier all contribute to drift. Humidity affects atmospheric refraction and can indicate approaching weather fronts. Wind speed in meters per second is the primary driver of high-frequency Dec error, as gusts push the optical tube off target. Barometric pressure in hectopascals completes the atmospheric model, affecting both refraction calculations and the density of the air column above the telescope.
body.p_15: The final group captures the temporal context of the tracking session. Error standard deviation over the last N cycles measures the current noise floor — whether conditions are stable or turbulent. Drift trend, computed as the slope of a linear regression fit to recent errors, captures the direction and rate of systematic drift. Innovation magnitude — the difference between the filter's prediction and the actual measurement — is a direct measure of how well the Kalman filter is performing. Residual norm provides a complementary measure of post-correction fit quality. Convergence indicator is a binary flag showing whether the filter has settled into a stable state. Session duration and plate solve count provide the ML model with context about how much data it has seen. Model age, the time since the last retrain, tells the system whether the model might be stale.
body.p_16: The noise scaling formulas are what make the ML layer actually useful. Without them, the ML model would predict drift values that the Kalman filter ignores, because the filter's noise parameters remain static. The scaling formulas translate ML predictions into filter tuning adjustments.
body.p_17: Process noise scale is computed as: clamp(1 + |drift| / baseline, 0.1, 10.0). When the ML model detects large systematic drift — perhaps a periodic error peak or a strong wind gust — the absolute drift value exceeds the baseline, and the scale increases above 1.0. This tells the Kalman filter to widen its process noise covariance, which means the filter will trust measurements more aggressively and correct faster. When drift is small and stable, the scale stays near 1.0, and the filter operates in its normal balanced mode.
body.p_18: Measurement noise scale is computed as: clamp(1 + scatter / baseline, 0.1, 10.0). When observations are noisy — poor seeing, thin clouds, or guide star confusion — the scatter term increases, and the measurement noise scale rises above 1.0. This tells the Kalman filter to widen its measurement noise covariance, which means the filter will trust its internal prediction model more and apply smaller corrections. This prevents the filter from chasing noise, which would introduce more error than it corrects.
body.p_19: Both scales are hard-clamped to the range [0.1, 10.0] to prevent two failure modes. If the scale drops to zero (covariance collapse), the filter ignores all information and the state estimate freezes. If the scale approaches infinity (covariance explosion), the filter trusts everything equally and the state estimate becomes dominated by the most recent noisy measurement. The hard clamp ensures neither extreme can occur, regardless of what the ML model predicts.
body.p_20: The hybrid system must operate safely across multiple threads without corrupting shared state. The WebServer thread calls PredictDriftML on every plate-solve cycle to retrieve the current ML predictions. The UI thread can trigger a manual ForceRetrain at any time. The EKF retraining runs on a background Task to avoid blocking the tracking loop. A modelLock mutex guards the engine swap — the moment a newly trained model replaces the old one, the lock ensures no thread reads a partially-initialized model.
body.p_21: Models persist to disk as zip archives: kalman_ml_model.zip for RA and kalman_ml_model_dec.zip for Dec. The current R² scores are written to metrics.json alongside the model files. When a fresh session starts, the system loads the saved model immediately, giving the filter ML augmentation from the first plate-solve cycle without waiting for a full training pass. The confidence gate ensures this works safely: if the saved model's R² is below 0.5 (perhaps because conditions have changed significantly since the last session), the system simply falls back to the raw Kalman filter until enough new data has accumulated for a fresh training pass.
body.p_22: Before the hybrid architecture was activated, the ML layer existed in the codebase but was effectively dead code. The confidence gate always returned 0.0, the engage condition never triggered, and the filter operated with static noise parameters — no different from a standard Kalman implementation. The difference after activation is stark.
body.p_23: With the ML layer active, the system consistently achieves sub-arcsecond tracking during long exposures. The process noise scaling allows the filter to respond aggressively to periodic error peaks while remaining calm during stable periods. The measurement noise scaling prevents the filter from chasing poor-seeing artifacts. And because the model retrains at the start of each session, it adapts to the specific conditions of that night — different temperature profiles, different wind patterns, different atmospheric stability — without requiring manual tuning by the observer.
body.p_24: The architecture is designed for extensibility. The 24-feature vector can be expanded with new sensors — a focuser temperature probe, a mount current sensor, a cloud camera — without modifying the ML engine. The SDCA regression can be replaced with a more sophisticated model — a gradient-boosted tree, a small neural network — without changing the four-step pipeline. And the noise scaling formulas can be tuned per-mount, per-OTA, per-climate, while the fundamental architecture remains unchanged. This is the essence of the hybrid approach: a clean separation between what the filter does and how the filter is tuned, connected by a learning layer that gets smarter with every session.
body.p_28: R² > 0.5: Model must prove itself before engaging
body.p_29: [0.1, 10.0]: Noise scale hard-clamped to safe range
body.p_32: Background Task: EKF retrains on background thread

article.headline: Altitude-Azimuth vs. Equatorial EKF
article.deck: Alt-az mounts need different Extended Kalman Filter tuning than equatorial mounts. HYBRID auto-adapts to both geometries — here is how and why.

sidebar.stat_desc: One 4-state model for equatorial mounts, one 6-state model for Alt-Az — each optimized for its coordinate geometry.

body.p_1: Elements tracking position, velocity, refraction, and field rotation simultaneously.
body.p_2: Nonlinear atmospheric correction updated every Kalman cycle.
body.p_3: Real-time Jacobian linearization of nonlinear mount geometry.
body.p_4: Machine learning noise tuning activates when model confidence is sufficient.
body.p_5: Alt-azimuth mounts are the underdog of precision astrophotography. Equatorial mounts sidestep the hardest problems by aligning one axis with Earth's rotation, reducing tracking to a single constant speed. Alt-az mounts offer no such luxury. Every axis moves at variable speed, field rotation twists the image across the sensor, and atmospheric refraction bends starlight in ways that change with altitude. For decades, the answer was simple: avoid the problem entirely and use an equatorial mount. HYBRID takes a different approach — it embraces the nonlinearities and solves them with an Extended Kalman filter.
body.p_6: Field rotation is the most visible problem. As an alt-az mount tracks a star across the sky, the field of view rotates around the optical axis. Stars do not trace straight lines across the sensor — they trace arcs. After just a few minutes of uncorrected tracking, stars become elongated streaks at the edges of the frame even while remaining sharp at the center. This effect is absent on equatorial mounts where the RA axis is aligned with the celestial pole.
body.p_7: Both axes must move simultaneously at variable speeds. On an equatorial mount, the RA axis spins at the sidereal rate — a constant 15.041 arcseconds per second — and the Dec axis sits still. On an alt-az mount, both altitude and azimuth change continuously, and the rate of change depends on where in the sky you are pointing. Near the zenith, azimuth speed approaches infinity. Near the horizon, atmospheric refraction becomes significant and nonlinear.
body.p_8: Atmospheric refraction compounds the problem. Stars near the horizon appear higher than they actually are, and the correction follows a secant-of-zenith-angle law — not a simple linear offset. The refraction coefficient changes with temperature, pressure, humidity, and wavelength. A static correction table gets you within a few arcseconds, but it cannot track the real-time drift that occurs as conditions shift through an imaging session.
body.p_9: Meridian crossing presents a discontinuity. When a target crosses the meridian, azimuth wraps from one side to the other. The mount must reverse direction on the azimuth axis while the altitude axis continues its smooth arc. Controllers that do not account for this wrap can produce tracking spikes or lost guide stars at exactly the wrong moment.
body.p_10: The traditional solution is to use an equatorial mount and avoid the problem entirely. HYBRID's approach is to model the nonlinearities explicitly and let the Extended Kalman filter handle them.
body.p_11: PHD2 uses a Proportional-Integral-Derivative controller — the workhorse of industrial control systems. The PID approach is simple, well-understood, and widely used. It measures the current error between the guide star's position and the target position, then applies a correction proportional to that error, its integral over time, and its rate of change.
body.p_12: For equatorial mounts, this works extremely well. The system is approximately linear: the mount moves at a constant speed on the RA axis, and corrections are small perturbations around that rate. PHD2's PID controller handles this regime with sub-arcsecond precision.
body.p_13: For alt-az mounts, the PID approach has fundamental limitations. There is no field rotation model — the controller treats the system as two independent linear axes when it is actually a coupled nonlinear system. There is no atmospheric refraction correction — the controller cannot distinguish between a tracking error and a refraction-induced offset. And the gains are static — they do not adapt to changing conditions as the target moves across the sky and the geometry shifts.
body.p_14: PHD2 is designed for guiding, not full tracking. It corrects small errors around an already-correct pointing solution. For alt-az mounts, that pointing solution must come from somewhere else.
body.p_15: MountWizzard4 fits periodic error to a harmonic series — a Fourier decomposition of the mount's systematic errors. This approach models the repeating patterns in a mount's motion as a sum of sinusoidal components at integer multiples of the sidereal rate.
body.p_16: For periodic error analysis, this is powerful. It reveals the specific harmonic content of a mount's error signature, allowing targeted correction. For well-behaved equatorial mounts, harmonic modeling can reduce periodic error to below one arcsecond.
body.p_17: But MountWizzard4 assumes that periodic error is the dominant error source. It does not model atmospheric refraction or field rotation. The harmonic model is per-mount — each mount needs its own calibration, and the model does not adapt in real-time to changing conditions. It is a characterization tool, not a tracking controller.
body.p_18: TPoint fits a polynomial surface to pointing measurements. By collecting dozens or hundreds of calibration points across the sky, TPoint builds a model of the mount's systematic pointing errors, including flexure, gear errors, and alignment offsets.
body.p_19: For pointing accuracy, TPoint is excellent. It can achieve arcsecond-class pointing across the entire sky after a thorough calibration run. The polynomial model captures spatial patterns that simpler models miss.
body.p_20: But TPoint is a static model. It does not adapt in real-time to temporal drift, atmospheric changes, or mechanical settling. It requires many calibration points — typically 30 to 100 — collected over multiple hours. And it is designed for pointing, not tracking. The model tells you where the mount should point, not how it should move between points.
body.p_21: HYBRID uses an Extended Kalman filter with a 6-element state vector: RA position, RA velocity, Dec position, Dec velocity, atmospheric refraction coefficient, and field rotation rate. This state captures the essential dynamics of alt-az tracking in a compact, computationally efficient form.
body.p_22: The EKF's Jacobian linearizes the nonlinear mount geometry at each time step. This handles the altitude-dependent azimuth speed, the secant-of-zenith-angle refraction law, and the field rotation geometry without requiring a globally linear model. The linearization is recomputed at every cycle, so it adapts automatically as the target moves across the sky.
body.p_23: The refraction coefficient in the state vector means the filter learns atmospheric conditions from the data itself. No external weather model is needed — the Kalman gain adjusts the refraction estimate based on how well the prediction matches the measurements. The field rotation rate is estimated directly from plate-solve solutions, so no external field rotation model is required.
body.p_24: A machine learning layer tunes the noise covariance matrices (Q and R) based on 24 features including seeing conditions, mount temperature, sky position, and session history. This layer engages when model confidence exceeds an R² threshold of 0.5, allowing the filter to optimize its own noise parameters without manual tuning.
body.p_25: The mathematical advantages of the Extended Kalman filter for alt-az tracking are structural, not incremental. The Kalman gain automatically balances prediction against measurement. When the model is confident, it trusts its prediction. When measurements are noisy, it relies more on the model. This balance is computed optimally in the minimum-variance sense — no heuristic tuning required.
body.p_26: The Jacobian linearization handles nonlinear terms at each step without requiring a globally linear model. The secant-of-zenith-angle refraction law, the altitude-dependent azimuth speed, and the field rotation geometry are all nonlinear functions that the Jacobian linearizes locally. This is fundamentally different from assuming the system is linear and hoping the errors are small.
body.p_27: The state includes the refraction coefficient directly, so the filter learns atmospheric conditions from the tracking data itself. No external weather station is needed. As temperature and pressure shift through the night, the refraction estimate adapts continuously. The field rotation rate is estimated from plate-solve solutions, so no external field rotation model is needed — the filter computes it from first principles.
body.p_28: No manual tuning is needed. The adaptive Kalman gain adjusts the filter's behavior based on observed noise characteristics. And the ML layer further optimizes the noise covariance matrices based on learned patterns from the current session. From the first plate solve, the filter is operational. No calibration session is required — the filter self-calibrates as it runs.
body.p_29: Honesty demands acknowledging where the other approaches are better. For simple equatorial guiding, PHD2 is the better choice. It is simpler, more established, and has a vast community of users and documentation. If you are using an equatorial mount and do not need alt-az tracking, PHD2 is the practical answer.
body.p_30: For periodic error analysis, MountWizzard4 is the specialized tool. Its harmonic decomposition reveals the specific frequency content of a mount's error in ways that a general-purpose tracker cannot match. Use MountWizzard4 to characterize your mount, then apply the results to your tracking strategy.
body.p_31: For pointing models, TPoint remains the gold standard. Its polynomial surface fitting with dozens of calibration points achieves pointing accuracy that general-purpose tools cannot match. If your primary need is accurate pointing across the sky, TPoint is the right tool.
body.p_32: HYBRID is the best choice for full alt-az tracking with machine learning enhancement. It handles the nonlinearities that the other tools ignore, adapts in real-time to changing conditions, and self-calibrates from the first plate solve. For alt-az mounts, it is not just the best option — it is the only option that addresses the full problem.
body.p_33: Measured tracking performance across the four approaches confirms the theoretical analysis. Equatorial EKF tracking achieves 0.5 to 1 arcsecond RMS — comparable to PHD2's 0.3 to 0.8 arcsecond RMS on equatorial mounts, with the EKF's advantage being that it adapts to conditions automatically while PHD2 requires manual gain tuning.
body.p_34: Alt-az EKF tracking achieves 1 to 2 arcsecond RMS with field rotation correction active. This is significantly better than what any of the other tools achieve on alt-az mounts, because none of them model field rotation explicitly. MountWizzard4 achieves periodic error correction within plus or minus 2 arcseconds, but this is periodic error only — it does not account for the other error sources that dominate alt-az tracking. TPoint achieves pointing accuracy within plus or minus 30 arcseconds, but this is a static pointing model, not a dynamic tracking solution.
body.p_35: RMS tracking error with Kalman filter on equatorial mount.
body.p_36: RMS tracking error with field rotation correction active.
body.p_37: RMS tracking error with PID controller on equatorial mount.
body.p_38: Pointing accuracy from polynomial surface model.
body.p_39: The numbers tell the story: HYBRID's alt-az EKF approach achieves tracking precision within a factor of two of dedicated equatorial guiding, while handling a fundamentally harder problem. For alt-az mounts, this is not incremental improvement — it is a new capability class.

article.headline: The Vision: Building a Self-Guiding Observatory
article.deck: Why a solo developer in France spent 18 months building the world's first voice-controlled telescope system with predictive AI tracking — and what it means for amateur astronomy.

sidebar.stat_desc: Kalman filter for reactive correction, Extended Kalman for nonlinear dynamics, ML layer for predictive adaptation.

body.p_1: From a frozen fingers problem to a production-hardened observatory system. 18 months. 200+ files. One vision: let the telescope think for itself.
body.p_2: There is a moment every amateur astronomer knows. It is -5°C, the dew is forming on the corrector plate, and you need to change the exposure time. You pull off your glove, find the tiny menu item, click it, type a number, click OK, and put your glove back on. By the time your fingers stop hurting, the seeing has changed and you need to do it all over again. This is how telescope control has worked for decades. This is the problem that started everything.
body.p_3: Every existing telescope control system is fundamentally reactive. You tell it where to point, and it points. You tell it to take a picture, and it takes a picture. If the mount drifts, you notice and correct. If the focus shifts, you notice and refocus. The human is always in the loop, always watching, always correcting. This works fine for visual observation, but for long-exposure astrophotography — where a single exposure might run for 10 minutes and a single imaging session might run for 8 hours — the reactive model breaks down.
body.p_4: Consider the problem of tracking accuracy. A telescope mount, even a good equatorial mount, drifts. Periodic error from the worm gear, flexure from temperature changes, atmospheric refraction, wind loading, polar alignment error — all of these contribute to drift that degrades image quality. The traditional solution is autoguiding: a second camera watches a guide star and sends correction pulses to the mount. This works, but it is reactive — the guide star must drift before the correction is applied, and the correction is always one cycle behind.
body.p_5: Guide star drifts → Camera detects error → Software computes correction → Pulse sent to mount → Mount corrects. Latency: 1-3 seconds. Error accumulates between corrections.
body.p_6: Plate solve measures position → Kalman filter predicts drift → ML layer adapts noise parameters → Correction applied preemptively → Mount stays on target. Latency: near-zero. Error prevented before it occurs.
body.p_7: The breakthrough insight was that no single tracking algorithm is sufficient. Atmospheric conditions change, mechanical properties shift with temperature, and different mount geometries require different mathematical models. HYBRID's solution is a three-layer architecture that combines passive, active, and predictive tracking:
body.p_8: This architecture was not designed in a vacuum. It was shaped by 18 months of real-world use, where each layer was validated, refined, and hardened against the actual conditions of amateur observatory operation. The Kalman filter's matrix dimensions were corrected. The EKF's correction pathway was completed. The ML layer's training pipeline was extended from single-axis to dual-axis regression. Each refinement was driven by a specific failure or limitation encountered in the field.
body.p_9: HYBRID does not reinvent hardware communication. It builds on the ASCOM Platform — the industry standard for amateur telescope control in Windows. ASCOM provides a unified API for mount control, camera operations, focuser positioning, filter wheel selection, and observatory automation. HYBRID's ASCOM controller wrapper (1,080 lines of C#) handles GOTO, slew, park, tracking, and pulse guide operations with full error handling and state management.
body.p_10: But ASCOM alone is not enough. The ASCOM standard defines how software talks to hardware — it does not define how software talks to AI. This is where the Model Context Protocol comes in. HYBRID exposes 47 telescope tools through an MCP server, making them available to any MCP-compatible AI assistant. Claude Desktop can discover these tools, understand their parameters, and chain them into complex sequences. The result is a system where the AI handles the orchestration while ASCOM handles the hardware.
body.p_11: Voice control is not a gimmick. It is a genuine usability improvement for the observing workflow. Consider the practical scenarios:
body.p_12: The Kalman filter predicts mount drift. The ML layer adapts to changing conditions. The scheduler optimizes target selection based on altitude, weather, and session history. The system reasons about its own state and makes decisions without human input.
body.p_13: ASCOM commands translate predictions into motion. The mount slews to targets. The camera captures exposures. The focuser maintains focus as temperature drops. The filter wheel selects the right filter for each target. The meridian flip executes safely with verification.
body.p_14: When clouds roll in, the scheduler pauses and waits. When the seeing improves, the ML layer tightens the noise parameters. When a target sets below the horizon, the system moves to the next target. When the session ends, the telescope parks safely and the system shuts down.
body.p_15: This is not science fiction. Every component exists today. The Kalman filter works. The ML layer adapts. The scheduler sequences. The voice pipeline dispatches. The ASCOM API commands. The remaining challenge is integration — making all these components work together reliably in the harsh conditions of a real observatory, where temperatures drop, clouds appear, and equipment must be trusted to operate unattended through the night.
body.p_16: The development roadmap reflects the progression from voice control to autonomous operation:
body.p_17: HYBRID Telescope Control is MIT-licensed and open to contribution. The source code is available for inspection, modification, and redistribution. This is deliberate: astronomical software benefits from transparency. When you are trusting software to control a $5,000 mount through the night, you want to be able to read the code that makes the decisions.
body.p_18: The project has been shaped by 20+ open-source projects: NINA's sequencer architecture, PHD2's guiding algorithms, Stellarium's sky chart rendering, Siril's image stacking, the OnStep community's hardware interface standards, and the MCP ecosystem's tool protocol. HYBRID stands on these shoulders, and the MIT license ensures it gives back.
body.p_19: HYBRID Telescope Control began as a simple question: what if the telescope could talk back? Not in the trivial sense of a wake-word assistant that responds to "Hey Telescope" — that is a toy that can handle "go to M31" but nothing more. The real question was: what if you could say "slew to M31, plate solve, center it, autofocus, and start imaging for two hours with 180-second exposures" — and the telescope just did it? What if the telescope could reason about the sequence, handle errors, and adapt to conditions without human intervention?
body.p_20: HYBRID takes a different approach: predictive tracking. Instead of waiting for the mount to drift and then correcting, HYBRID predicts the drift before it happens and applies corrections preemptively. The Kalman filter — the same algorithm that guides spacecraft and navigates submarines — estimates the mount's drift state from plate-solve measurements and projects it forward in time. The correction is applied before the drift occurs, not after.
body.p_21: Cold-weather operation. At -5°C, removing gloves to operate a laptop is painful and dangerous — frostbite can develop in minutes. Voice control lets you change targets, adjust exposures, and start imaging sequences without ever touching the keyboard.
body.p_22: Multi-tasking. While the telescope is imaging one target, you can verbally queue the next target, check the weather forecast, or adjust the scheduler — all without interrupting the current imaging session.
body.p_23: Accessibility. Observers with mobility limitations gain a more natural interaction model. Public outreach events become more engaging when you can demonstrate telescope control by speaking rather than clicking.
body.p_24: Complex sequences. The real power is in chaining commands. "Slew to M81, plate solve, autofocus, and start imaging for 30 minutes with 180-second exposures" — this is a single utterance that triggers four MCP tool calls, each dispatched to the ASCOM API. The AI handles the sequence logic; the hardware handles the motion.
body.p_25: The ultimate vision is not just voice control — it is autonomous operation. A self-guiding observatory that can:

article.headline: Kalman to OnStep: Real Code, Real Corrections
article.deck: The exact 5-step flow from Kalman state vector to OnStep pulse, with real Python and ASCOM code. Every line documented, every timing analyzed.

sidebar.stat_desc: From plate-solve capture to OnStep pulse delivery — the complete correction cycle runs in under 200 milliseconds.

body.h2_2: Step 1: Plate Solve — Measuring the Real Error
body.h2_3: Step 2: Kalman Filter — The Mathematical Engine
body.h2_4: Step 3: Python Converts to OnStep Commands
body.h2_5: Step 4: ASCOM Alpaca — The HTTP Bridge
body.h3_5: Method B: Custom Rates — Drift Compensation
body.p_1: Every five to fifteen seconds, the HYBRID Telescope Control system performs a remarkable sequence of events: a camera captures a star field, a plate solver determines the exact pointing position, a Kalman filter predicts future drift, Python converts those predictions into OnStep commands, ASCOM Alpaca transmits them over HTTP, and the mount firmware adjusts its tracking rate — all before the next image exposure begins. This article traces the complete communication path with real code.
body.p_2: The critical insight that emerges from reading the actual code is that the Kalman filter never communicates directly with OnStep. It performs pure mathematical computation in Python memory — updating state vectors, predicting future drift, and computing optimal tracking rates. A separate layer of Python code then translates those predictions into two distinct types of commands that are transmitted via ASCOM Alpaca's HTTP REST interface. Understanding this separation is essential to grasping why the system achieves ±0.5 arcsecond precision.
body.p_3: Every tracking cycle follows the same five-stage pipeline. The stages execute sequentially within a single loop iteration, and the entire sequence repeats every 5 to 15 seconds depending on the exposure time and plate-solve latency. Here is the flow at a glance:
body.p_4: The cycle begins with a camera exposure. The guide camera captures a 1-second image of the star field currently in the telescope's field of view. This image is handed to ASTAP, which performs plate-solving: matching the observed star patterns against its catalog to determine the exact Right Ascension and Declination the telescope is actually pointing at — not where it thinks it is, but where it truly is.
body.p_5: The plate-solve output produces two critical values: the solved coordinates (actual RA and Dec) and the measured error (the difference between the target position and the actual position, converted to arcseconds). This error is the raw material that feeds every subsequent step.
body.p_6: At this stage, we have a measured error in arcseconds. The telescope is off by -0.45 arcsec in Right Ascension and +0.32 arcsec in Declination. These numbers are passed directly to the Kalman filter.
body.p_7: This is the most misunderstood part of the pipeline. The Kalman filter runs entirely in Python memory. It does not open sockets, does not send HTTP requests, does not touch ASCOM. It is a pure mathematical function: given a measurement error and a time delta, update the internal state and compute the optimal tracking rate.
body.p_8: The state vector is four elements: RA position error, RA velocity (drift rate), Dec position error, and Dec velocity. On each cycle, three operations execute in sequence — update, predict, and compute optimal rate.
body.p_9: The update step fuses the new measurement with the filter's prior prediction. The Kalman gain matrix K determines how much weight to give the new measurement versus the existing state estimate. A high-confidence measurement pulls the state toward the observed error; a noisy measurement is trusted less.
body.p_10: The predict step projects the current state forward in time using the velocity estimates. If RA is drifting at -0.09 arcsec/sec, in 5 seconds the error grows to -0.45 + (-0.09 × 5) = -0.9 arcsec. This prediction is what makes Kalman more powerful than simple proportional correction — it anticipates where the error is going, not just where it is.
body.p_11: The optimal rate combines three factors: the base sidereal rate, the Kalman-predicted drift compensation, and a proportional correction based on the current error. The correction gain parameter (0.5 = 50%) controls how aggressively the system responds — higher values correct faster but risk oscillation.
body.p_12: Now the critical translation happens. The Kalman filter has computed an optimal tracking rate — a single number in arcsec/sec. Python must convert this into two distinct types of commands that OnStep understands. These are not alternatives; both are sent every cycle, in a specific order.
body.p_13: The SYNC command is a standard ASCOM operation. It does not use Kalman predictions at all — it simply tells OnStep the real coordinates. OnStep then recalculates its pointing model, correcting systematic errors like polar alignment drift.
body.p_14: Every command from Python reaches OnStep through ASCOM Alpaca's HTTP REST interface. There is no serial connection, no direct USB link — it is standard HTTP PUT requests to a local network endpoint. This makes the system network-accessible and protocol-agnostic.
body.p_15: The ASCOM Alpaca server runs on the same machine as OnStep, listening on port 11111. Python sends a PUT request with the command string in the body. ASCOM translates the LX200 protocol command to serial, sends it to OnStep's USB interface, receives the response, and returns it to Python as a JSON object.
body.p_16: The entire HTTP round-trip completes in approximately 50 milliseconds. Two additional PUT requests follow — one for the Dec offset, one for the SYNC coordinates — bringing the total ASCOM communication time to roughly 150 milliseconds for a complete correction cycle.
body.p_17: OnStep maintains these adjusted rates until the next correction arrives. Between plate-solve cycles, the telescope drifts at the corrected rate — which is why the Kalman prediction matters. A simple proportional correction would fix the current error but leave the drift uncompensated. Kalman's prediction of where the error will be in 5 seconds allows OnStep to track at exactly the right rate during the interval between corrections.
body.p_18: Here is the exact timeline of a single tracking cycle with timestamps, showing what happens at each moment:
body.p_19: The numbers tell the story. The first cycle measured -0.45 arcsec RA error. After Kalman corrections applied for 5 seconds, the next cycle measured only -0.05 arcsec. The system reduced error by 90% in a single cycle. With both SYNC and CUSTOM RATES working together, steady-state error settles to approximately ±0.5 arcseconds.
body.p_20: The most common question about this architecture is: why use two methods instead of one? The answer lies in what each method corrects. SYNC fixes the current position. CUSTOM RATES fixes the rate of drift. They solve different problems, and both are essential.
body.p_21: Consider what happens if you use only SYNC without custom rates. The position is corrected to zero error, but the mount continues tracking at the standard sidereal rate. Over the next 5 seconds, the same systematic drift that caused the original error rebuilds. By the next plate solve, the error has grown back to nearly the same value. SYNC alone is a reactive correction that fights the same battle every cycle.
body.p_22: Now consider custom rates without SYNC. The Kalman filter predicts drift and adjusts the tracking rate accordingly, but the initial position offset is never corrected. The mount tracks at a slightly different speed, but from the wrong starting point. Error decreases more slowly than it should.
body.p_23: Used together in the correct order — SYNC first to establish the correct position, then CUSTOM RATES to maintain it — the two methods create a tight feedback loop. SYNC resets the error to zero. Custom rates prevent it from growing. The result is steady-state tracking error of approximately ±0.5 arcseconds, sustained over hours of imaging.
body.p_24: The system uses a specific set of OnStep LX200 protocol commands, each serving a distinct purpose in the correction pipeline:
body.p_25: The system degrades gracefully when the Kalman filter is not available — for example, during initial startup before enough plate-solve measurements have accumulated, or if the kalman_filter module fails to import. In these cases, a simple proportional correction replaces Kalman's predictions:
body.p_26: The proportional fallback still sends both SYNC and CUSTOM RATES — the two-method approach is maintained regardless of whether Kalman is active. The difference is that the fallback reacts to the current error only, without predicting future drift. This yields roughly ±2 arcsecond precision compared to Kalman's ±0.5 arcsecond.
body.p_27: The filter is a pure mathematical function. It receives measurements and returns predictions. The translation to hardware commands happens in a separate Python layer.
body.p_28: SYNC corrects position. Custom rates correct drift. Both execute every cycle, always in the same order: SYNC first, then rates. The order matters — rates must be applied to the correct position.
body.p_29: Every command travels as an HTTP PUT request to a local network endpoint. There is no proprietary protocol, no direct hardware link. The ASCOM server translates HTTP to serial LX200 protocol for OnStep.
body.p_30: Plate solve: 2 seconds. Kalman computation: 1 millisecond. Three HTTP requests: 150 milliseconds. OnStep application: instantaneous. Total correction time: approximately 2.2 seconds within a 5-second cycle.
body.p_31: If Kalman is unavailable, the system falls back to proportional correction. If ASCOM is unreachable, the tracking loop continues with sidereal-only tracking. The mount never stops moving.
body.p_32:
ascom.sync_to_coordinates(ra, dec)

Tells OnStep: "You are at this position NOW." Corrects the position error. Realigns the pointing model. Instantaneous effect — fixes where the telescope thinks it is.
body.p_33:
ascom.command_string(":SXTR,offset#")

Tells OnStep: "Track at THIS speed." Compensates predicted drift. Continuous effect — adjusts how fast the telescope moves between plate solves.
body.p_34: This is where Kalman predictions become real motor commands. The :SXTR and :SXTD commands are OnStep-specific — they set RA and Dec tracking rate offsets relative to sidereal. A +0.068 arcsec/sec offset means OnStep tracks 0.068 arcsec/sec faster than sidereal, compensating the drift that Kalman predicted.
body.p_35: OnStep receives the LX200 protocol commands and applies them immediately to its tracking motor controller. The :SXTR command modifies the RA tracking rate by adding the specified offset to the base sidereal rate. The :SXTD command does the same for Declination.
body.p_36: The recommended commands are :SXTR and :SXTD (offsets) rather than :SX9A/:SX9B (absolute rates). Offset commands are additive — they adjust relative to the current rate — which means OnStep's own sidereal tracking remains active as the baseline. Absolute rate commands would override the sidereal baseline entirely, which can cause issues if the correction is momentarily interrupted.

article.headline: ML + Kalman: The Cognitive Layer
article.deck: The Kalman filter reacts. The ML layer predicts. Together they form a hybrid system that learns the telescope's personality — 24 features, SDCA regression, sub-arcsecond RMS.

sidebar.stat_desc: Position, temporal, environmental, and statistical features extracted from each plate-solve cycle feed the prediction model.

body.h2_1: The Fundamental Principle: From Estimation to Contextual Prediction
body.h2_3: How It Works: The Intelligent Control Loop
body.h2_4: The Concrete Example: Gear Oscillation at Dec 45°
body.h2_5: Fusion Formula: The Mathematics of Combination
body.h3_1: Layer 1: The Reflex System — Kalman Filter
body.h3_2: Layer 2: The Cognitive System — ML Predictive Model
body.h3_3: Step 1: Data Collection — Building the Learning Base
body.h3_4: Step 2: Model Training — SDCA Regression with Confidence Gate
body.h3_5: Step 3: Real-Time Integration — The Fusion Loop
body.p_1: The Kalman filter is a generalist model. It relies on simple physical equations — a constant-velocity model that assumes drift follows predictable, linear patterns. It is optimal for estimating the current state and the immediately future state, but it cannot learn or adapt to complex, recurring patterns specific to your mount, its position in the sky, or the ambient environmental conditions.
body.p_2: Machine Learning, by contrast, excels at discovering those complex patterns in historical data. An LSTM network can learn that "every time the telescope reaches Dec 45°, a gear oscillation occurs every 5 minutes" — a pattern no physics-based model could represent. The objective is to create a hybrid system that exceeds the limitations of each approach used alone.
body.p_3: The Kalman filter provides the foundation — real-time, mathematically grounded drift estimation with known convergence properties. The ML layer builds on top of that foundation — learning the specific mechanical personality of your mount over hundreds of hours of guiding data. Neither alone can achieve what the combination delivers.
body.p_4: The hybrid system operates on two distinct layers that run simultaneously, each handling a different timescale and a different class of tracking error.
body.p_5: The low-level Kalman filter continues to function exactly as before. Its role is to provide a smoothed, real-time estimate of the current drift — both position and velocity — by filtering measurement noise from plate-solve data. On equatorial mounts, a 4-state model (RA position, RA velocity, Dec position, Dec velocity) captures the essential linear dynamics. On Alt-Az mounts, a 6-state Extended Kalman filter handles the nonlinear coordinate transformation.
body.p_6: This layer operates on a ~5-second cadence, responding to each plate-solve measurement. It is the system's fast reflex — always running, always correcting, always mathematically optimal for the linear portion of the drift.
body.p_7: The high-level ML layer analyzes the Kalman filter's outputs over a longer time window — from several minutes to tens of minutes — along with contextual data about the mount state, environment, and correction history. Its purpose is to predict a more complex, future drift error that the Kalman filter's simple physics model cannot represent.
body.p_8: This layer is the system's cognition — it anticipates defects before they manifest. It learns that certain combinations of time, temperature, and telescope orientation produce specific mechanical behaviors, and it prepares corrections proactively rather than reacting after the fact.
body.p_9: For the ML model to function, it needs a rich database of contextual snapshots. At every guiding cycle — approximately every 5 seconds — the system records a snapshot containing 24 input features organized into four categories:
body.p_10: The prediction target — the label — is the future residual error. Specifically: "What will the guide sensor measure as drift in 60 seconds, despite the Kalman filter's standard corrections?" Once this future value is known, it becomes ground truth for training the model.
body.p_11: The model uses Stochastic Dual Coordinate Ascent (SDCA) regression — a principled optimization method that provides strong generalization guarantees. Training happens offline on hundreds of hours of recorded guiding data, where the model learns to associate contextual features with future drift error.
body.p_12: A critical design choice is the R² confidence gate. The ML model is only deployed if it demonstrates sufficient predictive power on unseen data. If the model's R² falls below a defined threshold — indicating it has not learned reliable patterns — the system gracefully degrades to Kalman-only tracking. This prevents an untrained or poorly trained model from injecting noise into the control loop.
body.p_13: Once trained and deployed, the ML model operates in parallel with the Kalman filter during live guiding:
body.p_14: Consider a concrete scenario. Your mount has a specific gear mesh pattern that causes a small oscillation every 5 minutes when the telescope is at a particular Declination.
body.p_15: The ML model predicts mechanical errors 60 seconds before they manifest, giving the control loop time to prepare and cancel the disturbance preemptively.
body.p_16: The fusion formula is deceptively simple, but its implications are profound. It represents the precise point where reactive control meets predictive intelligence.
body.p_17: The key insight is that C_K and C_ML operate on different timescales. C_K corrects what has already happened — the current drift error measured by the guide sensor. C_ML corrects what is about to happen — the predicted residual error that the Kalman filter's linear model cannot capture. The Gain parameter blends these two correction streams into a single command that addresses both the present and the near future.
body.p_18: The system does not stop learning after initial training. It continues to record data throughout every session, creating a continuous improvement cycle.
body.p_19: Every ML prediction — the predicted error at T+60 seconds — is compared against the actual measured error 60 seconds later. This prediction-versus-reality comparison generates new labeled training data. Over time, this data is used to periodically retrain the model, allowing it to adapt to mount changes, seasonal temperature variations, and new mechanical behaviors that emerge as the equipment ages.
body.p_20: This feedback loop is what makes the hybrid system genuinely adaptive. A pure Kalman filter is static — its physics model never changes. The hybrid system evolves, learning the unique personality of each mount and improving its predictions over weeks and months of operation.
body.p_21: Mathematically optimal for linear drift. Fast real-time response. Known convergence properties. Cannot learn mount-specific mechanical patterns. Limited to simple physical models.
body.p_22: Can learn complex patterns. Adapts to specific equipment. Requires large training datasets. No real-time guarantee. Risk of overfitting to training noise. Unstable without fallback.
body.p_23: Best of both: real-time reflexes + predictive intelligence. Graceful degradation to Kalman-only. Continuous learning loop. Adapts to mechanical changes over time.
body.p_24: Every 5 seconds: The Kalman filter processes the plate-solve measurement, smooths the noise, and estimates the immediate drift (position_kalman, velocity_kalman). Simultaneously, the ML model receives the sliding window of the last several minutes of feature data and produces a prediction: "Based on the patterns I have seen, I predict a residual error of X pixels in 60 seconds, even with standard Kalman corrections."
body.p_25: Fusion: Instead of sending only the Kalman correction to the mount, the system combines both sources. The Kalman correction (C_K) handles the immediate, short-term error. The ML correction (C_ML) provides a proactive counter-command to negate the predicted future error.
body.p_26: The Gain parameter controls how aggressively the ML correction is applied. A gain of 0 disables ML entirely. A gain of 1.0 applies the full predicted correction. Tuning this parameter balances responsiveness against stability.
body.p_27: Training session: The ML model analyzes historical data and discovers the pattern: when Dec ≈ 45° and session time ≈ 300 seconds, an oscillation of +0.8 pixels will occur 60 seconds later. This pattern repeats consistently across multiple sessions at the same Declination.
body.p_28: Live session: You are imaging at Dec 45°. The Kalman filter is tracking a normal linear drift. At minute 4:50, the ML model — seeing the current position and elapsed time — predicts: "Warning: in 10 seconds (at t=300s), an oscillation will begin. I predict an error of +0.8 pixels at t=360s."
body.p_29: At minute 5:00: The Kalman filter begins to see a small deviation — which it initially attributes to noise. The ML model, however, is confident. The command sent to the mount is not just the Kalman correction. It includes a small proactive counter-correction designed to cancel the predicted +0.8 pixel oscillation.
body.p_30: Result: The oscillation occurs, but it is immediately compensated by the proactive correction. The guide star remains perfectly centered. The Kalman filter is "surprised" to see almost no drift and barely updates its estimation. The mechanical defect that would have caused a visible star elongation in a long exposure is rendered invisible.
body.p_31: Complex mechanical compensation: The ML layer can learn and cancel mechanical defects — gear oscillations, periodic error harmonics, backlash patterns, thermal flexure — that the Kalman filter's simple physics model cannot represent. These are the errors that cause star elongation in long exposures.
body.p_32: Long-exposure improvement: By predicting and preemptively canceling periodic errors, the hybrid system maintains sub-arcsecond tracking accuracy over exposures that would otherwise show guiding artifacts. The improvement is most visible in exposures longer than 5 minutes.
body.p_33: Adaptability: The model improves with time. As more sessions are recorded, the ML layer learns the mount's evolving personality — seasonal thermal patterns, mechanical wear, new accessories that change the tube balance. The system gets better without manual retuning.
body.p_34: Complexity: The hybrid architecture is significantly more complex than a standalone Kalman filter. It requires training pipelines, feature engineering, model validation, and a fusion framework. The development and maintenance burden is substantially higher.
body.p_35: Training data requirements: The ML model needs hundreds of hours of high-quality, labeled guiding data to learn reliable patterns. Sessions with poor seeing, cloud cover, or equipment issues can introduce noise into the training set.
body.p_36: Overfitting risk: The model might memorize noise specific to the training sessions and fail to generalize to new conditions. The R² confidence gate mitigates this, but it remains a fundamental risk of any data-driven approach.
body.p_37: Stability concerns: ML corrections must be carefully bounded to prevent the proactive corrections from introducing new oscillations. The Gain parameter and correction clamping are essential safeguards against instability.

article.headline: Mount Modeling Reimagined: Plate-Solve, Kalman, and Auto-Alignment
article.deck: Traditional mount modeling collects dozens of calibration points over hours. HYBRID takes a different approach: the Kalman filter IS the pointing model — self-calibrating, real-time, and requiring zero manual intervention.

sidebar.stat_desc: Traditional pointing models need 30-100 star measurements. HYBRID self-calibrates from the first plate-solve.

body.h2_1: The Traditional Approach: TPoint and Polynomial Models
body.h2_2: HYBRID's Approach: The Kalman Filter as Pointing Model
body.h2_3: The SYNC Command: How the Model Updates
body.h2_6: How HYBRID Handles Common Error Sources
body.h2_8: When to Use Traditional Mount Modeling
body.h3_1: The State Vector as Pointing Model
body.p_1: Every telescope mount has a pointing model — a mathematical description of where the mount thinks it is pointing versus where it actually points. For a century, astronomers have built these models by measuring the positions of calibration stars, one by one, across the sky. The result is a polynomial surface that maps desired coordinates to actual mount positions. It works. It is accurate. And it requires hours of manual work that must be repeated whenever the mount moves, the temperature shifts, or the polar alignment drifts.
body.p_2: HYBRID Telescope Control does not use a traditional pointing model. Instead, it uses a plate-solve-based approach where the Kalman filter serves as a self-calibrating, real-time pointing model. Every 5 seconds, a plate-solve measures the actual pointing position. The Kalman filter estimates the error. A SYNC command tells the mount where it actually is. And the pointing model updates itself — continuously, automatically, and without any user intervention.
body.p_3: Traditional mount modeling works by collecting a set of calibration points across the sky. At each point, the user slews to a known star, measures the offset between where the mount thinks it is pointing and where the star actually is, and records the error. After collecting enough points — typically 30 to 100 — a polynomial surface is fitted to the error data. This surface becomes the pointing model: a lookup table that corrects for systematic errors like polar alignment offset, tube flexure, gear periodic error, and atmospheric refraction.
body.p_4: TPoint, developed by Software Bisque, is the gold standard for this approach. It uses a sophisticated polynomial fitting algorithm that can achieve arcsecond-class pointing accuracy across the entire sky. Professional observatories use TPoint variants for their mount calibration. The approach is well-understood, well-proven, and well-documented.
body.p_5: But polynomial models have inherent limitations. They are static: the model does not change after calibration. If the temperature shifts and the tube flexes differently, the model is wrong. If the polar alignment drifts over a long session, the model is wrong. If the mount is moved to a different location, the model must be rebuilt from scratch. The model captures spatial patterns — how pointing errors vary across the sky — but not temporal patterns — how pointing errors change over time.
body.p_6: HYBRID replaces the static polynomial model with a dynamic Kalman state vector that is continuously updated by plate-solve measurements. The Kalman filter estimates four quantities for equatorial mounts: RA position error, RA velocity error, Dec position error, and Dec velocity error. These four states capture the essential pointing dynamics — where the mount is off and how fast the error is changing.
body.p_7: For Alt-Az mounts, the state vector extends to six elements: Az position error, Az velocity error, Alt position error, Alt velocity error, and two bias terms that capture the nonlinear coupling between the axes. The Extended Kalman filter handles the coordinate transformation through a numerical Jacobian, allowing the same Kalman framework to work for both mount geometries.
body.p_8: The Kalman state vector is not just a tracking correction — it IS the pointing model. The four (or six) state elements describe the mount's current pointing error in a compact, mathematical form. The state captures both the current offset and the rate of change, allowing the filter to predict where the mount will be in 5 seconds and correct before the error occurs.
body.p_9: This is fundamentally different from a polynomial model. A polynomial tells you "at RA 6h, Dec +30°, the mount is off by +12 arcseconds in RA." The Kalman state tells you "right now, at whatever position you are, the mount is off by +12 arcseconds in RA, and the error is increasing at +0.3 arcseconds per second." The Kalman model is position-independent and time-adaptive.
body.p_10: The SYNC command is the bridge between measurement and model. When a plate-solve determines the actual pointing position, the SYNC command tells the mount: "You are here now." OnStep (the mount controller) then recalculates its internal pointing model, correcting systematic errors.
body.p_11: In HYBRID's architecture, the SYNC command is issued after every Kalman correction cycle. The flow is: plate-solve captures the actual position → Kalman computes the error and the correction → the correction is applied as a MoveAxis command → the SYNC command updates the mount's internal model. This creates a closed-loop system where the pointing model is continuously refined by real measurements.
body.p_12: A traditional calibration session involves slewing to specific stars, centering them in the eyepiece, recording the offset, and repeating 30-100 times. Each measurement is a static snapshot. The entire session takes 1-3 hours.
body.p_13: HYBRID's SYNC approach is continuous and automatic. Every plate-solve is effectively a calibration measurement — but instead of being recorded and processed later, it is applied immediately through the Kalman filter. The mount is continuously self-calibrating throughout the entire imaging session.
body.p_14: HYBRID's auto-alignment system combines plate-solving with the Kalman filter to achieve polar alignment without user intervention. The approach is based on the TVC (Takahashi Variation Correction) method, adapted for plate-solve-based measurement.
body.p_15: At session start, HYBRID takes a short exposure (3-5 seconds), plate-solves the image to determine the exact pointing coordinates, and compares them to the expected position. The offset between expected and actual position reveals the mount's initial pointing error — a combination of polar alignment error, tube alignment error, and any mechanical offset.
body.p_16: The system then slews to a second star, plate-solves again, and computes the pointing error at the new position. By comparing the error vectors at two different positions, the system can separate polar alignment error (which produces a characteristic pattern of RA/Dec offsets across the sky) from other error sources.
body.p_17: The process repeats iteratively: slew, plate-solve, measure offset, compute correction, SYNC. Each iteration refines the alignment. After 3-5 iterations, the system converges to within ±5 arcseconds of polar alignment — comparable to what an experienced astronomer achieves with a dedicated polar scope and 30 minutes of manual adjustment.
body.p_18: After the initial alignment, the Kalman filter takes over. Every subsequent plate-solve further refines the pointing model. Over the course of a long imaging session, the pointing accuracy improves as the Kalman filter accumulates more data about the mount's behavior at different positions, temperatures, and hour angles.
body.p_19: Polar alignment is the foundation of equatorial mount pointing. If the polar axis is not aligned with the celestial pole, every pointing model must compensate for the resulting field rotation — a systematic error that grows with hour angle and produces characteristic star trails in long exposures.
body.p_20: HYBRID implements a plate-solve-based polar alignment routine modeled on the TVC method. The algorithm measures the pointing offset at two positions in RA, computes the polar alignment error from the differential offset, and provides a correction vector that the user (or an automated focuser) can apply to the mount's azimuth and altitude adjusters.
body.p_21: The accuracy of ±5 arcseconds is achieved through the same plate-solve precision that powers the tracking system. The plate solver uses star pattern matching with sub-arcsecond centroid accuracy, providing the measurement precision needed for precise polar alignment.
body.p_22: Every mount has a characteristic set of error sources. Here is how HYBRID's dynamic approach handles each one, compared to traditional static modeling.
body.p_23: Polar alignment error produces a field rotation that depends on hour angle. A static model captures this at the time of calibration. HYBRID's Kalman filter tracks it continuously — as the hour angle changes, the filter's Dec velocity state adapts to the changing field rotation rate.
body.p_24: Periodic error is a sinusoidal wobble that repeats once per worm revolution. Traditional models capture the dominant harmonic. HYBRID's Kalman filter tracks the full periodic error waveform — including higher harmonics and amplitude variations that change with temperature and load.
body.p_25: Tube flexure changes with telescope orientation — pointing straight up produces different flexure than pointing at the horizon. A static model captures the average flexure.
