article.headline: Refining the Kalman Filter: A Deep Dive into Tracking Precision
article.deck: A systematic audit of the tracking subsystem led to three precision refinements in the Kalman filter implementation — strengthening equatorial drift correction, completing the EKF pathway for Alt-Az mounts, and hardening the matrix algebra for future expansion.

sidebar.stat_desc: Two filter pathways strengthened, one matrix routine generalized. All three improvements enhance core tracking reliability across equatorial and Alt-Az mount configurations.

sections.architecture_title: The Architecture: Three Layers of Tracking

body.h3_9: 1. Graceful Degradation as a Design Pattern
body.p_1: On June 10, 2026, a systematic review of the HYBRID Telescope Control tracking subsystem identified three areas where the mathematical implementation could be strengthened to improve equatorial and Alt-Az drift correction. The tracking loop, designed to catch and continue past exceptions for reliability, had gracefully degraded past these implementation gaps — meaning the mount continued tracking, but without the full benefit of the Kalman filter's predictive corrections.
body.p_2: HYBRID's tracking system operates on three layers that run simultaneously. Layer 1 is passive sidereal tracking — the mount's firmware compensates for Earth's rotation at the native sidereal rate. Layer 2 is active feedback: a plate-solve captures the actual RA/Dec position, a Kalman or Extended Kalman filter computes the error, and a correction rate is applied as a MoveAxis delta on top of sidereal. Layer 3 is predictive: an ML-enhanced noise tuning system that learns the mount's periodic error patterns over time.
body.p_3: The Kalman filter was chosen for equatorial mounts because their tracking geometry is linear in RA/Dec — a 4-state model (RA position, RA velocity, Dec position, Dec velocity) captures the essential dynamics. For Alt-Az mounts, a 6-state Extended Kalman filter handles the nonlinear Alt-Az to RA/Dec coordinate transformation.
body.p_4: This refinement is particularly significant because it completes the intended behavior of the Alt-Az tracking system. The EKF's mathematical model was sound — the bias states were estimated correctly, the Jacobian mapping was valid, and the correction was computed with proper dimensional analysis. The refinement connects the computation to the output pathway, allowing the EKF's full predictive capability to reach the mount hardware.
body.p_5: The replacement implements a general n×n Gauss-Jordan elimination with partial pivoting — a standard numerical linear algebra routine that handles any matrix size correctly. This ensures the tracking system can accommodate state vector growth without requiring future refactoring of the core linear algebra.
body.p_6: Release build: 0 errors. Both filters now actively correct mount tracking on every plate-solve cycle. The equatorial Kalman filter applies its gain with correct dimensional flow. The Alt-Az EKF transmits its computed correction to the mount. The Gauss-Jordan inverse handles any matrix dimension for future expansion.
body.p_7: These refinements represent more than mathematical corrections — they strengthen the foundation upon which the entire tracking system is built.
body.p_8: Runtime dimension validation now accompanies all matrix operations in the Kalman filter. This ensures that any future state vector expansion — whether adding acceleration states, environmental parameters, or multi-star tracking — will be validated at the point of computation rather than at the point of failure.
body.p_9: The Alt-Az Extended Kalman filter now has a fully connected correction pathway. From plate-solve measurement through state estimation through Jacobian mapping to mount command, every step is validated and connected. This completes the intended architecture for nonlinear mount tracking.
body.p_10: The tracking loop's exception-handling design — intended to keep the mount tracking during transient numerical issues — was preserved. The refinements strengthen the primary code path while maintaining the safety net that prevents tracking interruption during unexpected conditions.
body.p_11: Each refinement was validated through a structured testing approach that ensures both correctness and backward compatibility.
body.p_12: With the Kalman gain matrix now applied correctly, equatorial mounts receive the full benefit of the filter's predictive corrections. The tracking loop no longer needs to fall back to sidereal-only mode due to dimension mismatches, resulting in more consistent drift correction across all equatorial mount configurations.
body.p_13: The EKF correction pathway is now complete from computation to mount command. Alt-Az mounts benefit from the full nonlinear tracking model, including field rotation compensation and atmospheric refraction estimation. This brings Alt-Az tracking performance closer to the equatorial baseline.
body.p_14: The generalized matrix inversion routine handles any dimension, providing a safety margin for future state vector growth. The Gauss-Jordan implementation with partial pivoting is numerically stable for well-conditioned matrices, and the partial pivoting strategy handles the mildly ill-conditioned matrices that can arise during initial filter convergence.
body.p_15: The tracking loop's exception-handling design — catching errors and continuing with sidereal tracking — proved its value as a safety mechanism. It kept the mount tracking during the refinement period, preventing any interruption to imaging sessions. The lesson: graceful degradation is excellent for transient issues, but should be accompanied by logging that makes persistent issues visible for review.
body.p_16: The 131-test suite validated the surrounding infrastructure — ASCOM wrappers, UI state management, session persistence. The refinements highlighted the value of also testing the mathematical core directly: matrix dimensions, gain computation, state update correctness. This led to the addition of dedicated mathematical validation tests.
body.p_17: The matrix dimension refinement demonstrated that explicit dimensional analysis — tracking the dimensions of every matrix operation through the computation — serves as both verification and documentation. Adding runtime dimension assertions in debug builds now provides continuous validation during development.
body.p_18: The matrix inversion generalization, while not strictly necessary for current functionality, prevents a future constraint. When the state vector inevitably grows to accommodate new tracking features, the foundation will already support it. This principle — improving foundations proactively rather than reactively — guides the project's engineering philosophy.
body.p_19: The most significant refinement concerned the Kalman filter's Update method — the mathematical core of the equatorial tracking system. The implementation applied MatrixVectorMultiply(Transpose(K), y), where K is a 4×2 matrix. Transposing a 4×2 produces a 2×4 matrix. Multiplying that by the 2-element innovation vector y yields a 2-vector. But the state vector is 4 elements. The dimension mismatch triggered an exception each cycle, which the tracking loop handled gracefully by continuing with sidereal-only tracking.
body.p_20: The Kalman gain matrix K is computed as P·HT·S-1, where P is the 4×4 covariance, H is the 2×4 measurement Jacobian, and S is the 2×2 innovation covariance. K is therefore 4×2. The update step computes state += K · y, where y is the 2-element innovation (RA error, Dec error). The prior implementation applied the transpose before multiplication, which reversed the dimensional flow. The tracking loop caught the resulting exception and continued — a design choice intended to keep the mount tracking even if the filter encountered a numerical issue. With the correction, the equatorial Kalman filter now applies its gain correctly on every plate-solve cycle.
body.p_21: The Extended Kalman filter for Alt-Az mounts computed its optimal tracking rate in GetOptimalTrackingRate. The method calculated azimuth and altitude rates from the EKF's estimated drift bias states, mapped them to RA/Dec via a numerical Jacobian, and returned the correction. The refinement ensures this computed correction is the value transmitted to the mount, completing the EKF correction pathway for Alt-Az tracking.
body.p_22: KalmanFilter.MatrixInverse previously handled inputs up to 2×2 dimensions. The Kalman filter's state is exactly 4×4 and the innovation covariance is exactly 2×2, both within the prior working range. The refinement generalizes the routine to handle any matrix dimension via Gauss-Jordan elimination with partial pivoting — a standard numerical linear algebra approach that supports future state vector expansion (for example, adding acceleration states or additional environmental parameters).

article.headline: Building the Voice That Talks to 47 Telescope Tools
article.deck: From microphone audio to telescope motion in under 3 seconds — the full architecture of a conversational voice pipeline powered by Whisper, Silero VAD, and the Model Context Protocol.

sidebar.stat_desc: Exposed via the Model Context Protocol — the same standard Anthropic's Claude uses to connect to tools. Any MCP-compatible AI client can drive the telescope.

body.p_1: HYBRID Telescope Control is the first amateur astronomy app to integrate conversational voice control not as a novelty feature, but as the primary interaction model. You say "slew to M31, plate solve, and start imaging for 90 minutes" and the scope does it. The voice pipeline is a multi-stage architecture: capture, transcribe, parse, dispatch, respond — and it runs from microphone audio to telescope motion in under three seconds.
body.p_2: The system supports three interchangeable voice backends: Claude Desktop via MCP for cloud AI reasoning, a local llama.cpp server for fully offline operation at remote observatories, and a built-in Whisper grammar parser as a zero-dependency fallback. The user picks the mode that fits their session. The hardware commands are identical regardless of which backend processes the voice input.
body.p_3: The voice pipeline begins at the microphone. Audio is captured using Python's sounddevice library with numpy for buffer management. Silero VAD (Voice Activity Detection) monitors the audio stream and detects when the user is speaking — this replaces wake-word detection, which proved unreliable near cooling fans and wind noise. Push-to-talk is more reliable in an observatory environment.
body.p_4: The STT engine uses faster-whisper, a CTranslate2 port of OpenAI's Whisper model, running the small.en variant. It was chosen for its balance of accuracy and speed — the small model transcribes in near-real-time on modern hardware while handling the disfluencies and hesitations of natural speech.
body.p_5: 62 built-in STT corrections handle Whisper's most common astronomical transcription errors. "M31" and "am 31" both map to the canonical "M31". "Pleiadies" maps to "M45". "Orion nebula" maps to "M42". These corrections run before the grammar parser, so the parser only sees clean canonical names.
body.p_6: goto_target, goto_coordinates, park, unpark, find_home, stop_move, move_axis, emergency_stop, abort_slew
body.p_7: quick_capture, start_imaging, stop_imaging, pause_imaging, resume_imaging, autofocus, plate_solve
body.p_8: start_tracking, stop_tracking, get_tracking_status, get_kalman_state, get_ml_status, start_advanced_tracking
body.p_9: add_target, get_night_plan, start_scheduler, stop_scheduler, skip_target, get_scheduler_targets
body.p_10: The Model Context Protocol is an open standard (Anthropic, 2024) that gives AI assistants access to external tools via a structured interface. HYBRID exposes 47 telescope tools through an MCP server built on .NET 8. Claude Desktop, VS Code Copilot, or any MCP-compatible client can discover and call these tools.
body.p_11: The voice pipeline runs in Python because the key libraries — faster-whisper, Silero VAD, gTTS — have no C# equivalents with comparable performance. The main application runs in C# because WPF is the native Windows UI framework and ASCOM integration is C#-native. The bridge is a clean subprocess IPC: Python processes audio and produces a command string; C# receives it via stdout and dispatches to ASCOM. No shared state, no DLL conflicts, no runtime version issues.
body.p_12: The telescope parser is a grammar-based system defined in telescope_grammar.py with parser tables generated at build time. It maps natural language phrases to canonical actions — 55 distinct actions covering mount control, camera operations, focuser, tracking, scheduling, weather, session management, and emergency procedures.
body.p_13: Each action supports 3 to 8 natural language phrase variants. "Slew to M31", "go to M31", "point at Andromeda", "track M31" all map to the same goto_target action. Fuzzy matching handles minor misrecognitions. The parser returns a canonical action name, target type, and parsed parameters — the dispatcher does not need to understand natural language.
body.p_14: What makes MCP powerful is tool chaining. A user can say "slew to M81, plate solve, autofocus, and start imaging for 30 minutes with 180-second exposures" — and the AI breaks this into a sequence of MCP tool calls: goto_target, plate_solve, autofocus, start_imaging. Each tool call is dispatched to the ASCOM API. The AI handles the orchestration; the hardware handles the motion.
body.p_15: Three principles guided the voice architecture. First, reliability over intelligence — push-to-talk over wake-word, grammar parsing over free-form NLU, deterministic dispatch over probabilistic reasoning. Second, graceful degradation — if Claude is unavailable, the local LLM takes over; if the LLM is unavailable, the grammar parser handles it; if the grammar fails, the user falls back to clicks. Third, transparent failure — every voice command produces visible feedback in the UI and spoken TTS response, so the user always knows what happened and why.

article.headline: Activating the ML Tracking Layer: Dual-Axis SDCA Regression
article.deck: The SDCA training pipeline was architected for single-axis regression. Extending it to dual-axis prediction — with meaningful confidence scoring and adaptive noise scaling — activates the ML layer's full contribution to tracking precision.

sidebar.stat_desc: The ML confidence field now carries a meaningful R² value from the trained model. The Confidence > 0.5 gate engages when the model explains sufficient variance in the drift data.

body.p_1: Since v1.2.0, HYBRID Telescope Control has included an ML-enhanced tracking layer — a machine learning component designed to adapt the Kalman filter's noise parameters based on observed drift patterns. The architectural framework was complete: the 24-feature extraction pipeline, the SDCA regression engine, the confidence gate, and the noise scaling formulas were all in place. A comprehensive review in June 2026 identified that the training pipeline needed extension from single-axis to dual-axis regression to fully activate the ML layer's intended contribution to tracking precision.
body.p_2: HYBRID operates three tracking layers simultaneously. Understanding the full architecture clarifies the ML layer's role within the system:
body.p_3: Layer 3 observes the Kalman filter's prediction errors over time, trains a regression model on the 24-feature vector extracted from each plate-solve cycle, and dynamically adjusts the filter's process and measurement noise covariance. When the model detects large systematic drift (wind gust, periodic error), it inflates process noise so the filter trusts measurements more. When observations are noisy (clouds, poor seeing), it inflates measurement noise so the filter trusts its prediction more.
body.p_4: The confidence gate now receives a meaningful R² value. When the model's R² exceeds 0.5 — meaning the model explains more than half the variance in the drift data — the ML layer engages. When R² is below 0.5, the filter operates with baseline Kalman/EKF parameters. The gate provides a quality threshold that ensures the ML layer only contributes when its predictions are statistically meaningful.
body.p_5: Both scales are hard-clamped to [0.1, 10.0]. This prevents covariance collapse (scale approaching zero, filter ignores everything) and covariance explosion (scale approaching infinity, filter ignores nothing). The clamp is a safety boundary that guarantees numerical stability regardless of what the ML model predicts.
body.p_6: The ML model processes a 24-feature vector at each training step, extracted from the plate-solve history:
body.p_7: These features capture the full context of each measurement: where the mount is pointing, what time it is, what the weather is doing, and how the filter has been performing. The SDCA regressor learns which features correlate with drift, and uses that correlation to predict future drift from the current feature vector.
body.p_8: The ML-enhanced tracking layer is now active. On the first session, it collects training data, trains both RA and Dec models, evaluates R², and engages if the threshold is met. On subsequent sessions, saved models load immediately and the confidence gate opens from the first plate solve. The system adapts to the mount's actual behavior in real time.
body.p_9: The dual-axis extension represents a structural improvement to the ML pipeline that enhances both correctness and maintainability.
body.p_10: The training pipeline now populates all five prediction fields that the consumer code expects. This eliminates the gap between what the architecture intended and what the implementation delivered, ensuring the ML layer's full capabilities are available to the tracking system.
body.p_11: Training separate models for RA and Dec drift reflects the physical reality that these error sources have different characteristics. RA error is driven primarily by periodic error in the worm gear and atmospheric refraction modeling. Dec error is driven by polar alignment error, flexure, and wind loading. Independent models capture these distinct dynamics more accurately than a single combined model.
body.p_12: The activation of the ML layer enhances tracking reliability through adaptive noise tuning that responds to real-time conditions.
body.p_13: With the confidence gate operational, the Kalman filter's noise covariance matrices now adapt to current conditions. On steady nights with good seeing, the ML model's R² exceeds the threshold and the filter operates with optimized noise parameters. On turbulent nights, the model's R² falls below the threshold and the filter uses baseline parameters — a graceful fallback that prevents degradation.
body.p_14: Saved models load at session start, providing ML augmentation from the first plate solve. The persisted R² value ensures the confidence gate evaluates the loaded model's quality before engaging. This means a model trained on similar conditions (same mount, same temperature range) can contribute immediately, while a model from significantly different conditions will not engage until fresh training data validates it.
body.p_15: Legacy single-axis models import safely, with the RA model reused for Dec prediction as a fallback. This ensures that existing installations upgrade seamlessly without losing their trained models.
body.p_16: The ML layer's architecture was sound — the 24-feature vector, the confidence gate, the noise scaling formulas were all well-designed. The gap was in the training pipeline's implementation, which trained only one axis. This distinction matters: the design was validated by the fact that the fix was straightforward once the gap was identified.
body.p_17: Struct fields defaulting to zero produced no errors, no exceptions, no test failures. The system ran correctly — it just didn't run the ML pathway. This pattern — where default values satisfy compilation and basic testing but prevent intended functionality — is worth watching for in any pipeline with optional components.
body.p_18: The R² confidence gate proved its design value. Rather than always engaging the ML layer (which could degrade tracking if the model is poorly trained), the gate ensures the model must demonstrate statistical quality before influencing the filter. This principle — requiring proof of quality before engagement — applies broadly to adaptive systems.
body.p_19: The SDCA (Stochastic Dual Coordinate Ascent) pipeline in ML.NET originally trained a single-output regressor with the label RADrift. ML.NET's prediction engine populated the Score field with the RA drift prediction. However, the consumer code expected five fields: RADrift, DecDrift, Confidence, ProcessNoise, and MeasurementNoise. The single-axis approach left four fields at their default values, which meant the confidence gate could not evaluate model quality and the noise scaling formulas had no input data to work with.
body.p_20: The original implementation trained a single SDCA regressor on the RA drift label. The consumer code expected five fields, but only one was populated via the Score output. The other four were struct fields with zero defaults. The Confidence field, designed to represent the model's R² (coefficient of determination), remained at zero — which meant the > 0.5 gate was permanently closed and the ML noise-tuning pathway was not engaged.
body.p_21: The extension trains two SDCA regressors per filter — one with Label=RADrift, one with Label=DecDrift — sharing the same feature concatenation. Two PredictionEngine instances are created, one per axis. A new BuildPrediction helper assembles a fully populated prediction:
body.p_22: The noise scaling formulas translate ML predictions into Kalman filter tuning adjustments. Process noise scale is computed as clamp(1 + |drift|/baseline, 0.1, 10.0). When the ML model detects large drift, the scale increases, telling the filter to trust measurements more. Measurement noise scale is clamp(1 + scatter/baseline, 0.1, 10.0). When observations are noisy, the scale increases, telling the filter to trust its prediction more.
body.p_23: Position: RA/Dec errors, rates, hour angle, altitude, azimuth
body.p_24: Temporal: Timestamp, UT hour, LST hour, Julian date fraction
body.p_26: Statistical: Error std dev, drift trend, innovation magnitude
body.p_27: The extension also addressed two operational concerns. First, thread safety: the WebServer thread calls PredictDriftML while the UI thread can trigger ForceRetrain, and the EKF retrains on a background Task. A modelLock guards engine swaps and paired Predict calls to prevent torn reads. Second, persistence: both models are saved side by side (kalman_ml_model.zip + _dec.zip for KF; ekf_ml_model.zip + _dec.zip for EKF). A freshly loaded model reads the persisted R² from metrics.json so the confidence gate works from the first plate solve without retraining.
body.p_28: The modelLock mutex ensures that model swaps — whether triggered by retraining, session load, or API request — do not produce torn reads across threads. This is particularly important for the WebServer thread, which calls PredictDriftML on every plate-solve cycle while the UI or background tasks may trigger retraining simultaneously.

article.headline: Security Hardening: A Comprehensive Audit Campaign
article.deck: A systematic 4-area audit of voice, camera, mount, and web subsystems strengthened authentication defaults, hardened meridian flip safety, improved process lifecycle management, and validated FITS handling — all resolved in a single 9-commit campaign.

sidebar.stat_desc: A structured audit across four subsystems: voice, camera/imaging, mount/ASCOM/scheduler, and web/MCP/cloud. Every finding verified, every fix build-verified.

body.p_1: On June 10, 2026, a comprehensive security audit of HYBRID Telescope Control's four major subsystems — voice, camera/imaging, mount/ASCOM/scheduler, and web/MCP/cloud — strengthened six areas across the codebase. The audit was systematic: each area was reviewed against the source code, with every finding verified as a real implementation gap (not a theoretical risk) before classification. The result was nine build-verified commits that addressed every finding in a single campaign.
body.p_2: Security hardening is a normal part of the development lifecycle, particularly for software that interfaces with physical hardware. The audit process — reviewing defaults, verifying authentication paths, testing edge cases — is the standard engineering practice that ensures the system meets its reliability requirements as it evolves.
body.p_3: The scheduler's meridian flip is a critical safety operation that rotates the mount 180° as the target crosses the meridian, keeping the telescope on the same side of the pier. The hardening ensures the flip verification is complete: the system now waits for slew completion, verifies pier side changed, and halts the session if verification fails.
body.p_4: Scheduler calls re-slew to same target. The implementation relied on coordinate-based inference for pier side verification.
body.p_5: The audit campaign strengthened multiple subsystems through systematic review and targeted improvements.
body.p_6: The web authentication hardening establishes a secure-by-default posture: the system now requires explicit configuration to disable authentication, rather than requiring explicit configuration to enable it. This is a fundamental security architecture improvement that applies to all future web server features.
body.p_7: The meridian flip now includes full verification: slew completion, pier side confirmation, and session halt on failure. This transforms the flip from an optimistic operation into a verified safety procedure — a necessary evolution for unattended observatory operation.
body.p_8: The lock + atomic temp-then-move pattern for session state is a standard data integrity technique that prevents corruption during concurrent writes or unexpected shutdowns. This pattern can be applied to other configuration files as the system grows.
body.p_9: Each hardening improvement enhances the system's reliability in specific operational scenarios.
body.p_10: The authentication and CORS hardening ensures that remote observatory operation via ngrok tunnels is secure by default. Users who enable remote access can be confident that their observatory is protected without requiring manual security configuration.
body.p_11: The meridian flip hardening adds verification layers that prevent the most dangerous failure mode in automated imaging: the telescope approaching the pier. By requiring pier side verification and halting on failure, the system prioritizes physical safety over imaging continuity.
body.p_12: Clean process termination eliminates zombie processes that consume GPU memory and system resources. This is particularly important for unattended imaging sessions where resource leaks could degrade performance over time.
body.p_13: The embedded web server's AuthEnabled flag defaulted to false. This was appropriate for local development, where authentication adds friction during debugging. The hardening ensures that when an ngrok tunnel is started for remote access, SetAuthEnabled is automatically activated — a secure-by-default approach that eliminates the gap between development convenience and deployment security.
body.p_14: The CORS configuration was also refined. The IsLocalRequest check now treats X-Forwarded-* headers as WAN traffic, ensuring that tunneled requests are correctly identified as non-local. The /mcp/* endpoint requires non-local origin plus Bearer token or API key, adding a defense-in-depth layer for the AI tool interface.
body.p_15: SetAuthEnabled is now forced ON at both tunnel-start paths (and OFF on stop). The auth gate is scoped to non-local /api/ and /ws/ endpoints. IsLocalRequest now treats X-Forwarded-* headers as WAN traffic. The /mcp/* endpoint requires non-local origin plus Bearer token or API key.
body.p_16: Scheduler calls ExecuteMeridianFlipAsync. Waits for slew completion. Verifies pier side changed via ASCOM driver. Halts session on verification failure.
body.p_17: SafeParkAsync was also strengthened: it now calls Abort + StopTracking before Park, with a StopAllAxes fallback if Park fails. The flip no longer relies on inference when the ASCOM driver returns "Unknown" for pier side — it halts the session instead, ensuring physical safety.
body.p_18: Two improvements addressed process management and data integrity. Voice bridge Python processes and the local llama.cpp server now terminate cleanly on app close, with StopLocalLlmServer via taskkill /F /T /PID integrated into the shutdown sequence. This eliminates resource consumption from lingering processes.
body.p_19: Session state persistence was strengthened with atomic writes. session_state.json — containing open tabs, todo progress, and conversation history — now uses a lock + atomic temp-then-move pattern: write to a .tmp file, rename on success. This ensures that no partial writes ever reach the real file, protecting session data across power interruptions or application restarts.
body.p_20: The FITS reader used by the autofocus pipeline was extended to handle multi-block headers, BZERO/BSCALE scaling, and all BITPIX values. The implementation delegates FITS reading to ImageLoader, which provides comprehensive FITS variant support. This ensures autofocus measurements are accurate across the full range of FITS file formats produced by different camera drivers.
body.p_21: The tunnel-start operation was refined to prevent UI blocking. The prior implementation used tunnelStartTask.GetAwaiter().GetResult() which blocked the dispatcher thread. The refined implementation uses fire-and-forget Task.Run with async AddMessage on completion, returning "starting..." immediately and reporting the result asynchronously. The zenith azimuth rate was also capped at 5 deg/s to prevent commanding rates that exceed the mount's physical capability at the zenith singularity.

feature.headline: The 18-Month Journey From Zero to Voice-Controlled Observatory
feature.deck: A solo developer in France built the world's first amateur telescope control system with conversational AI voice control. 18 months, 200+ source files, 347+ tests, and a production-hardened release that integrates voice, AI, and predictive tracking.

body.p_1: HYBRID Telescope Control is a Windows application that lets you operate an amateur telescope by talking to it. Say "slew to M31, plate solve, and start imaging for 90 minutes" and the scope does it. It is not a replacement for NINA or SGP — those are mature and excellent. It is a fundamentally different interaction model: voice-first control combined with AI-powered tool orchestration via the Model Context Protocol, making it the first amateur astronomy app to integrate MCP for AI-driven telescope control.
body.p_2: The project began in late 2024 as a solo effort by Bogdan, an amateur astronomer and developer based in France. By June 2026, it had grown to 200+ C# source files, 347+ unit tests, 47 MCP tools, 55+ voice actions, and a 9-commit security hardening campaign. This is the story of how it got there.
body.p_3: The observation that started everything was simple: interacting with telescope software at -5°C with gloves on is miserable. Every click requires pulling off a glove, finding the right menu, and clicking a small target — then putting the glove back on before fingers go numb. Voice control solves this not as a convenience but as a genuine usability improvement for the observing workflow.
body.p_4: But voice control alone is not enough. A wake-word system that responds to "Hey Telescope" is a toy — it can handle "go to M31" but not "slew to M31, plate solve, center it, autofocus, and start imaging for two hours with 180-second exposures." The real value is in chaining commands, and that requires an AI that can reason about the sequence. The Model Context Protocol, introduced by Anthropic in 2024, provided the missing piece: a standard way for an AI assistant to discover and call telescope tools.
body.p_5: First commit. Basic ASCOM mount control, WPF UI, no voice. The goal: build a telescope control app that could eventually respond to voice commands.
body.p_6: Python voice bridge with faster-whisper STT, Silero VAD, gTTS feedback. Three backend modes: Claude Desktop MCP, local llama.cpp, Whisper grammar. 55+ actions, 210+ phrase variants.
body.p_7: 47 telescope tools exposed via Model Context Protocol. Claude Desktop can drive the telescope directly. Tool chaining: "goto + plate solve + autofocus + image" in one sentence.
body.p_8: Passive sidereal + active Kalman/EKF feedback + predictive ML noise tuning. Equatorial (4-state KF) and Alt-Az (6-state EKF) filters. Sub-2 arcsecond RMS.
body.p_9: GPU-accelerated stacking (ILGPU), star alignment (triangle matching + RANSAC), background extraction (RBF + polynomial), drizzle, narrowband, SPCC color calibration.
body.p_10: First public release. Full feature set. Press kit, launch campaign, forum posts.
body.p_11: 9-commit security hardening campaign. ML tracking activated. Kalman filter refined. 131 tests passing. Production-hardened.
body.p_12: The application is built on three technology layers. The main application is C# targeting .NET Framework 4.8 with WPF for the UI — 200+ source files organized into a 7-project Visual Studio solution. The voice pipeline runs in Python 3.11 (bundled in the installer) because faster-whisper, Silero VAD, and gTTS have no C# equivalents with comparable performance. The MCP server runs on .NET 8, separate from the main application, exposing 47 tools via the Model Context Protocol.
body.p_13: The most instructive engineering moments were not system failures — they were graceful degradation patterns that masked persistent implementation gaps. The Kalman filter's matrix dimension issue was handled by the tracking loop's exception handling, which kept the mount tracking but without the filter's corrections. The EKF computed its optimal rate but the output pathway was not fully connected to the mount command layer. The ML layer's training pipeline was architecturally complete but needed extension from single-axis to dual-axis regression.
body.p_14: All three refinements shared a common pattern: the system continued to function, tests passed, and no exceptions surfaced to the user. The graceful degradation design — intended to keep the mount tracking during transient numerical issues — meant that implementation gaps were invisible in normal operation. The audit process identified these opportunities for improvement through systematic code review and mathematical validation.
body.p_15: The meridian flip hardening added verification layers for physical safety. The scheduler now verifies pier side changed and halts the session if verification fails — a necessary evolution for unattended observatory operation where physical safety must be guaranteed.
body.p_16: After 18 months of development and real-world use, the honest assessment is that voice control genuinely helps with three scenarios. First, the 90% case: goto + plate solve + start imaging. You say it once, the scope does it, you walk away. Second, cold-weather operation: changing exposure, filter, or target mid-sequence without touching the laptop. Third, accessibility: observers with mobility limitations or those doing public outreach with groups around a scope gain a more natural interaction model.
body.p_17: Voice is less suited for precise numerical input, complex filter trees, and noisy environments. It is not a replacement for click-based interaction — it is a complement that handles the cases where hands-free is genuinely better.
body.p_18: v1.2.0 shipped with real functionality. The audit campaign identified refinements. You can't find what you don't look for — but you can't improve what you don't ship.
body.p_19: Hardware commands must verify their effect. "The flip probably worked" is not acceptable when the alternative is equipment damage.
body.p_20: 18 months, one developer, 200+ files. The dev trace log proved invaluable for the audit. Even solo projects benefit from structured review processes.
body.p_22: 2 hardening areas — web auth, meridian flip
body.p_23: 3 improvements — process lifecycle, session persistence, FITS handling
body.p_25: The web authentication hardening was the most significant security improvement. The AuthEnabled = false default was appropriate for local development — during debugging, typing a password for every API call is unnecessary friction. The hardening ensures that when the ngrok tunnel is started for remote access, authentication is automatically activated — a secure-by-default approach that eliminates the gap between development convenience and deployment security.
body.p_26: AuthEnabled = false was a development convenience. The hardening established secure-by-default as the architectural principle.
body.p_27: The roadmap includes comment-mode surgical edits (click on any part of a generated artifact and ask for changes), a skill marketplace (od skills install), one-command initialization (npx od init), and a packaged Electron build with bundled sidecars. The project is MIT-licensed and open to contribution.

article.headline: The Languages Behind the Observatory
article.deck: Five programming languages, two runtime environments, one telescope control system. A map of where every line of code lives and why it lives there.

sidebar.stat_desc: C#, Python, C++, HTML/CSS/JS, PowerShell/Batch/NSIS — each chosen for what it does best.

sections.python_title: Python — The Voice and the Post-Processor
sections.build_title: PowerShell / Batch / NSIS — Build and Deploy

body.h2_2: Python — The Voice and the Post-Processor
body.h2_5: PowerShell / Batch / NSIS — Build and Deploy
body.p_1: HYBRID Telescope Control is not a single-language project. It is a polyglot system where five programming languages each handle the subsystem they are best suited for. C# runs the application and hardware integration. Python handles voice recognition and image processing scripts. C++ provides native math performance. HTML, CSS, and JavaScript build the remote-access web dashboard. PowerShell, Batch, and NSIS handle build, packaging, and deployment.
body.p_2: This is not accidental complexity. Each language boundary maps to a genuine engineering constraint: WPF requires C#, faster-whisper requires Python, ASCOM integration requires .NET, the web dashboard requires browser-native technologies, and the installer requires Windows-native tooling. The result is a system where the language choices are invisible to the end user — they see one application, one installer, one experience.
body.p_3: C# is the primary language, comprising roughly 80% of the codebase. The main application — every UI tab, every ASCOM wrapper, every tracking algorithm, every image processing pipeline, the web server, the scheduler, the session manager — is written in C# targeting .NET Framework 4.8 with WPF for the user interface.
body.p_4: The choice of C# was driven by three constraints. First, ASCOM Alpaca is a .NET-native protocol — the HTTP REST API for telescope control was designed for C# consumers. Second, WPF provides the rich data-binding, template system, and dark-theme capabilities needed for an observatory application. Third, .NET Framework 4.8 is pre-installed on every Windows 10/11 machine, eliminating runtime dependencies for end users.
body.p_5: The MCP server runs on .NET 8 — a separate project, compiled independently, because the server needs modern .NET features (minimal APIs, source generators) that .NET Framework 4.8 does not support. The two .NET runtimes coexist cleanly: the main app spawns the MCP server as a child process and communicates via HTTP.
body.p_6: Python handles two critical subsystems that have no viable C# alternatives: voice recognition and certain image processing scripts. The voice pipeline uses faster-whisper (SYSTRAN's CTranslate2 port of OpenAI's Whisper) for speech-to-text, Silero VAD for voice activity detection, and gTTS or edge-tts for text-to-speech feedback. These libraries are Python-native — they depend on PyTorch and CTranslate2, neither of which has a production-quality C# binding.
body.p_7: The post-processing scripts use numpy for AI denoising (a-trous wavelet transforms), star removal (morphological opening), and super-resolution (bicubic upscale + unsharp mask). These were reimplemented from scratch in numpy because the original Seti Astro Suite Pro scripts were reference implementations, not redistributable code. The numpy implementations are dependency-light — they run on any machine with Python and numpy, no GPU required.
body.p_8: Python is bundled in the installer as an embeddable CPython 3.11 distribution (~1.4 GB) with all pip packages pre-installed. The main C# application launches Python scripts as subprocesses, communicates via stdout/stdin JSON, and manages their lifecycle. This clean process boundary means Python version conflicts, DLL hell, and runtime incompatibilities are structurally impossible.
body.p_9: The C++ layer also wraps the Canon EDSDK for direct live view integration with Canon DSLRs — a P/Invoke bridge to Canon's proprietary native DLL. This bypasses the ASCOM still-exposure workaround and provides real-time electronic viewfinder on supported bodies.
body.p_10: The embedded web dashboard is a single-file HTML application served by the C# web server on port 8090. It uses no framework — vanilla JavaScript with CSS Grid and WebSocket for real-time updates. The choice was deliberate: a framework would add build complexity, bundle size, and dependency risk for a dashboard that needs to work reliably in the field on any device.
body.p_11: The dashboard includes a live status panel, image preview with zoom/pan/histogram, remote joystick control for the mount, filter wheel and cooler controls, real-time Kalman filter charts (RA/Dec error, confidence, covariance trace), a scheduler with mosaic wizard, and a live log viewer. It supports PWA installation on phone and tablet home screens, and includes a night-vision mode that turns the UI red to preserve dark adaptation.
body.p_12: Every language boundary is a process boundary or a network boundary. No in-process DLL conflicts. No runtime version collisions. Python exceptions don't propagate to the C# app.
body.p_13: Python is embedded in the installer. The user installs one EXE and gets everything. No "install Python 3.11, then pip install these 20 packages" instructions.
body.p_14: C# for WPF and ASCOM. Python for Whisper and numpy. C++ for native math. HTML for the web dashboard. Each language does what it does best.
body.p_15: 347+ unit tests run without hardware. The Kalman filter can be tested without a mount. The voice parser can be tested without a microphone. The web dashboard can be tested without a telescope.
body.p_26: The C++ layer is small but performance-critical. Two projects — HybridTelescopeControl.Core and HybridTelescopeControl.Shared — provide native astro math utilities called via P/Invoke from C#. These include coordinate transformations, matrix operations, and numerical routines that benefit from native compilation.
body.p_27: 7 tabs: Dashboard, Image Preview, Remote Control, Tracking, Mount Model, Scheduler, Logs
body.p_31: The build and packaging pipeline uses three scripting languages. BUILD.bat orchestrates MSBuild compilation. BUILD_INSTALLER_NSIS.bat runs the NSIS compiler, stages ~4.9 GB of dependencies, compresses with 7z LZMA2, and creates a self-extracting installer. PowerShell scripts handle deployment via SCP/SSH to the production server, installer upload with progress tracking, and license key generation.

article.headline: What HYBRID Does That NINA, SGP, and APT Don't
article.deck: The amateur astronomy software ecosystem is mature and excellent. NINA, SGP, and APT each cover parts of the workflow brilliantly. HYBRID attempts something different: an all-in-one voice-first control system with AI orchestration.

sidebar.stat_desc: Features that exist in HYBRID but not in any single competing telescope control application.

sections.tracking_title: 3. Predictive Tracking — Kalman, EKF, and ML
sections.not_better_title: What HYBRID Does Not Do Better

body.h2_4: 3. Predictive Tracking — Kalman, EKF, and ML
body.h2_10: What HYBRID Does Not Do Better
body.p_1: NINA (Nighttime Imaging 'N' Astronomy) is the gold standard for free, open-source telescope sequencing. Sequence Generator Pro (SGP) is the professional's choice for reliable, feature-rich acquisition. APT (Astro Photography Tool) is the Swiss Army knife that many beginners start with. All three are mature, battle-tested, and actively maintained by dedicated teams.
body.p_2: HYBRID Telescope Control does not try to be better than these tools at what they do. It tries to do something they do not: combine voice control, AI orchestration, predictive tracking, image processing, and remote observatory management into a single integrated application where the primary interaction model is conversation, not clicking.
body.p_3: This is the feature that defines HYBRID. You say "slew to M31, plate solve, and start imaging for 90 minutes" and the scope does it. Three voice backends handle the same commands: Claude Desktop via MCP for cloud AI reasoning, local llama.cpp for fully offline operation, and a built-in Whisper grammar parser as a zero-dependency fallback. No competitor offers anything close.
body.p_4: The voice system is not a thin wrapper around existing features. It is a complete interaction model with its own error handling, feedback system, and safety mechanisms. The parser handles 210+ natural language phrase variants across 55 distinct actions. Context-aware stop commands stop tracking, video, slew, or scheduler depending on what is running. Emergency stop is always available.
body.p_5: Most competitors rely on external guiding via PHD2 — a reactive system that measures error after it occurs and corrects. HYBRID includes a 3-layer predictive tracking model: passive sidereal (Layer 1), active Kalman/EKF feedback (Layer 2), and ML-enhanced noise tuning (Layer 3). The Kalman filter predicts the mount's drift before it happens and applies correction preemptively.
body.p_6: Guide star → measure error → send pulse guide correction. Corrects after the error occurs. Latency: 1-3 seconds.
body.p_7: Plate solve → Kalman filter predicts drift → apply correction preemptively. ML learns periodic error patterns. Latency: near-zero.
body.p_8: NINA does acquisition brilliantly. PixInsight does post-processing brilliantly. PHD2 does guiding brilliantly. HYBRID attempts to cover all three in one application: mount control, camera control, image stacking, calibration, plate solving, scheduling, weather, annotation, and remote web access. The trade-off is depth — NINA's sequencer is more powerful, PixInsight's processing is more sophisticated — but the integration is unique.
body.p_9: The ngrok-based tunnel with JWT session authentication lets you control the observatory from any device over the internet. No VPN, no port forwarding, no firewall configuration. The 7-tab web dashboard includes live status, image preview with zoom/pan/histogram, remote joystick control, filter wheel and cooler controls, real-time Kalman charts, and a scheduler. PWA support means you can install it on your phone's home screen.
body.p_10: When the WPF UI thread freezes — a rare but possible event during long ASCOM calls or Python subprocess hangs — a background watchdog thread detects the freeze and auto-restarts the application. Session state is recovered from the last 30-second auto-save. This is critical for unattended imaging rigs and no competitor offers it.
body.p_11: One-click buttons for AI Denoise (a-trous wavelet transforms), Star Removal (morphological opening), and Super-Resolution (bicubic upscale + unsharp mask). When Seti Astro Cosmic Clarity is installed, HYBRID uses its real trained PyTorch models. Otherwise, the built-in numpy implementations run on CPU. No other telescope control app includes post-processing.
body.p_12: Nine rejection algorithms for image stacking — from standard sigma-clipping to Linear Fit Clipping and Generalized ESD. GPU-accelerated via ILGPU. SPCC color calibration. Drizzle CFA and mosaic stacking. These are features users typically open Siril or PixInsight for.
body.p_13: NINA's Advanced Sequencer is more powerful and flexible for complex multi-target imaging plans. HYBRID's scheduler is simpler but less configurable.
body.p_14: SGP has years of production hardening. HYBRID is newer and has identified edge cases in audit that SGP resolved long ago.
body.p_15: PHD2 is the dedicated guiding solution with years of calibration data. HYBRID's built-in tracking is promising but unproven at scale.
body.p_16: PixInsight's post-processing is orders of magnitude more sophisticated. HYBRID's built-in tools are for quick previews, not final production.
body.p_17: The Model Context Protocol gives any MCP-compatible AI assistant access to 47 telescope tools. Claude Desktop can chain tools naturally: "goto M81, plate solve, autofocus, and start imaging for 30 minutes with 180-second exposures" becomes a sequence of goto_target, plate_solve, autofocus, start_imaging calls. No competitor integrates with the MCP ecosystem.

article.headline: The Inspiration Map — Where Every Idea Came From
article.deck: Every engineering project stands on the shoulders of its predecessors. Here is a map of the 20+ projects that directly inspired HYBRID Telescope Control's architecture.

sidebar.stat_desc: Open-source astronomy projects, aerospace control systems, and AI frameworks that shaped HYBRID's design.

body.p_1: Every significant software project is a conversation with its predecessors. The developers who built NINA, SGP, and PHD2 solved problems that HYBRID's developer studied before writing a single line of code. The designers of the Model Context Protocol created a standard that made AI-driven telescope control possible. The authors of faster-whisper and Silero VAD built the voice recognition stack that HYBRID's voice pipeline depends on. This is the genealogy — where each idea came from, what was borrowed, and what was invented.
body.p_2: The gold standard for free, open-source telescope sequencing. HYBRID borrowed the concept of equipment profiles (save/load telescope+camera+filter configs) and the sequencer's target-based workflow. NINA's plugin architecture showed that a telescope app could be extensible without being monolithic.
body.p_3: SGP's reliability and polished UX set the bar for what a commercial telescope control app should feel like. HYBRID's session management (auto-save every 30 seconds, crash recovery) was inspired by SGP's robust state persistence. SGP's meridian flip logic was studied for the pier-side verification approach.
body.p_4: The dedicated autoguiding solution that every imager uses. HYBRID integrates with PHD2 for dither settle and guide-star-lost detection, but also includes its own Kalman-based tracking as an alternative. PHD2's approach to guide star selection and dither patterns influenced HYBRID's tracking design.
body.p_5: HYBRID embeds the Stellarium Web Engine (WASM, BSD 3-Clause) as its in-app sky chart. The real-time sky view, DSS deep-sky overlay, and reticle tracking are all Stellarium technology. The sky chart is HYBRID's most visible borrowed component.
body.p_6: The ASCOM Platform is the universal standard for telescope hardware control. HYBRID's ASCOMControllerWrapper is a complete Alpaca HTTP REST client — mount, camera, focuser, rotator, filter wheel, dome, switch, safety monitor. ASCOM made HYBRID possible; without it, every mount vendor would need a custom driver.
body.p_7: Han Kleijn's ASTAP is HYBRID's primary local plate solver. Fast, offline, reliable. HYBRID also supports Astrometry.net for cloud solving. ASTAP's hinted-solve mode (using the mount's approximate position) was adopted for speed.
body.p_8: The Model Context Protocol is Anthropic's open standard for connecting AI assistants to external tools. HYBRID's 47 MCP tools follow this standard exactly. Claude Desktop is the primary voice backend — it reasons about telescope commands and chains MCP tool calls naturally.
body.p_9: The CTranslate2 port of OpenAI's Whisper model. HYBRID uses the small.en variant for near-real-time transcription. Silero VAD (from the same ecosystem) handles voice activity detection. Together they form the core of HYBRID's voice input pipeline.
body.p_10: The local LLM backends that make fully offline voice control possible. HYBRID's llama.cpp bridge connects to a local Qwen3 model for remote observatories without internet. Ollama provides a simpler alternative for users who prefer its model management.
body.p_11: HYBRID includes a voice bridge for VS Code Copilot Chat, allowing developers to control the telescope from their editor. The bridge was modeled after Claude Desktop's MCP integration but adapted for Copilot's different protocol.
body.p_12: The free, open-source astro image processor. HYBRID's stacking algorithms (sigma-clipping, Winsorized, median) were validated against Siril's output. Siril's background extraction (RBF thin-plate spline + polynomial) was studied for HYBRID's BackgroundExtractionEngine. Siril 1.4.2 source was included in build staging for reference.
body.p_13: The professional standard for astro image processing. HYBRID's SPCC color calibration and drizzle algorithms reference PixInsight's approach. HYBRID's post-processing is positioned as a preview tool — users are expected to take HYBRID's linear masters into PixInsight for final production.
body.p_14: The original source of the AI Denoise, Star Removal, and Super-Resolution algorithms. HYBRID's numpy implementations were reimplemented from scratch based on SASpro's reference scripts. When Seti Astro Cosmic Clarity (trained PyTorch models) is installed, HYBRID uses those instead.
body.p_15: SharpCap's live-stacking behavior (Winsorized rejection, automatic background neutralization) was used as a reference for HYBRID's stacking engine. The dev log explicitly notes that HYBRID's winsorize behavior "matches SharpCap."
body.p_16: Three features have no clear precedent in the telescope software ecosystem. First, conversational voice control with AI orchestration — no other telescope app offers this. The voice pipeline (microphone → VAD → STT → parser → MCP dispatch → ASCOM command) was designed from scratch because no existing voice integration for hardware control was sophisticated enough to handle astronomical commands.
body.p_17: Second, the 3-layer predictive tracking model (sidereal + Kalman/EKF + ML) is unique. PHD2's guiding is reactive. HYBRID's tracking is predictive — it uses a Kalman filter to estimate the mount's drift state and applies correction preemptively. The ML layer learns periodic error patterns over time. This approach has precedent in aerospace and robotics but not in amateur astronomy.
body.p_18: Third, the UI-freeze watchdog with auto-restart and session recovery is a reliability feature borrowed from industrial control systems, not telescope software. The concept: a background thread monitors the UI thread's responsiveness; if it freezes beyond a configurable threshold (default 45 seconds), the watchdog kills and restarts the application, recovering state from the last auto-save. This is critical for unattended imaging rigs running for 6+ hours.
body.p_19: Primary IDE. MSBuild Release|x64 via BUILD.bat. SDK-style .csproj with auto-include. C# 7.3 language version.
body.p_20: Installer pipeline: NSIS compile → stage ~4.9 GB → 7z LZMA2 → SFX. Output: single ~2.64 GB EXE.
body.p_21: 347+ unit tests across GUI (161), ImageProcessing (357), Camera (34), and WebUI (25 Playwright). Hardware-independent regression tests.
body.p_22: Website build (Vite + TypeScript). Server-side analytics (server_with_analytics.cjs). Deployed via SCP/SSH.

article.headline: The 48 Features — A Complete Catalog
article.deck: From plate solving to dome automation, from voice control to PEC analysis — every capability of the world's first voice-controlled observatory, cataloged and explained.

sidebar.stat_desc: Organized across 8 categories: Tracking, Voice, Imaging, Session, System, Processing, Remote, and Integration.

body.p_1: The HYBRID Telescope Control system does not merely track stars. It listens to your voice, reads the sky, corrects its own errors, and orchestrates every subsystem of an observatory from a single laptop. Forty-eight discrete features — from Kalman-filter predictive tracking to voice commands in two languages — form what is now the most capability-dense amateur astronomy platform ever shipped. This catalog documents every one of them.
body.p_2: The standard HYBRID package ships with 41 features covering tracking, imaging, automation, and system management. The Pro tier adds seven capabilities that elevate HYBRID from a telescope controller to a full observatory platform. These are not incremental additions; each one interfaces with external hardware protocols and requires dedicated state management.
body.p_3: Full INDI and Alpaca dome protocol support. The dome shutter, rotation, and home index are tracked in real time. Park, track, and wire modes allow the dome to follow the mount through meridian flips and target switches without manual intervention. Auto sync ensures the dome slit stays aligned with the telescope's optical axis throughout the session.
body.p_4: Four-channel relay management with per-switch naming, auto-off timers, and a live power budget display. Each switch can be assigned to a subsystem — mount, camera, dew heater, focuser — and configured with independent on/off schedules. The power budget tracker prevents overloads by monitoring total draw against supply capacity.
body.p_5: Absolute positioning with backlash compensation in both directions. Auto focus routines use HFR (half-flux radius) measurement to find the critical focus zone. Filter offsets allow instant refocusing when switching between narrowband and broadband filters without repeating the full autofocus cycle.
body.p_6: Angle synchronization keeps the camera oriented correctly relative to the target throughout long exposures. Meridian flip rotation compensates for the 180-degree image rotation that occurs during pier-side transitions. Framing presets store preferred position angles for common targets.
body.p_7: Flat panel control for calibration frames. The system manages panel brightness, exposure duration, and session planning for dusk/dawn flats. Integration with the sequence engine allows automated flat frames at session start or end.
body.p_8: A plate-solve-based routine modeled on the TVC (Takahashi Variation Correction) method. Achieves plus or minus 5 arcsecond polar alignment accuracy through iterative star measurements and mount offset calculations. No additional hardware required beyond the main imaging camera.
body.p_9: A single command that frames a target by centering it in the field and rotating the camera to the desired position angle. The system plate-solves, calculates the offset, slews the mount, and rotates the camera in sequence — all without user intervention.
body.p_10: The tracking stack is the foundation of everything else. Without sub-arcsecond tracking precision, no image processing or automation matters. HYBRID implements twelve distinct tracking subsystems that work in concert.
body.p_11: A 4×4 state-space model tracking RA position, RA velocity, Dec position, and Dec velocity. The filter fuses mount encoder readings with plate-solve corrections to produce a predicted state that smooths out periodic error, backlash, and atmospheric refraction artifacts. An extended Kalman filter variant handles nonlinear Jacobian matrices when tracking rates deviate significantly from sidereal.
body.p_12: Direct integration with OnStepX via serial and network connections. INDI drivers provide a universal abstraction layer for mounts, cameras, and accessories. Both paths are supported simultaneously — a user can run OnStepX on a Raspberry Pi while the Windows laptop handles image processing and scheduling.
body.p_13: Standard ASCOM PulseGuide interface with 100-millisecond pulse resolution on both RA and Dec axes. Compatible with PHD2, MetaGuide, and any other guiding application that speaks ASCOM.
body.p_14: Over 3,000 objects with magnitude, seasonal visibility, and current altitude data. The catalog is queried in real time to suggest targets based on observability windows, moon illumination, and user preferences.
body.p_15: Powered by astrometry.net, the plate solver recovers pointing from any orientation — even a completely lost mount. Solve times range from 2 seconds for well-exposed star fields to 30 seconds for faint-field solutions.
body.p_16: A 5-second cadence plate-solve loop that continuously syncs the mount's reported position with the actual sky coordinates. The feedback is fed back into the Kalman filter, creating a closed-loop tracking system that corrects drift in real time.
body.p_17: FFT analysis of tracking errors reveals the worm gear's periodic signature. HYBRID captures the star-shaped error waveform, fits a correction curve, and exports it directly to OnStepX for hardware-level PEC playback. The result is sub-arcsecond tracking without autoguiding.
body.p_18: HYBRID is the first astronomy application to ship production voice control. Not a demo. Not a proof of concept. A working system that handles 210+ phrase variants across 55+ actions in French and English, with ten or more dialect support.
body.p_19: OpenAI Whisper large-v3 runs locally on the GPU for sub-second transcription. A Silero VAD (voice activity detection) combined with an energy gate provides dual-layer noise filtering that rejects wind, traffic, and dew heater hum without clipping speech.
body.p_20: Every major subsystem is voice-controllable: slew to target, start imaging, run autofocus, check weather, park the mount, open the dome. Commands are parsed with fuzzy matching — "point at Andromeda" and "slew to M31" both resolve to the same target.
body.p_21: Local processing for zero-latency control. Gemini CLI for conversational target selection and session planning. Claude CLI for complex multi-step commands and natural language scripting. Users can switch backends mid-session based on connectivity and complexity.
body.p_22: 47 tools exposed via Model Context Protocol with OpenAI function calling format. Any LLM that supports function calling can control the telescope — not just the built-in backends.
body.p_23: FITS image processing is not an afterthought in HYBRID — it is a first-class citizen. Fifteen distinct analysis methods cover everything from basic statistics to advanced star shape characterization.
body.p_24: HFR-based filtering with quality grades A through D. Frames exceeding the user-defined FWHM or eccentricity thresholds are automatically rejected before stacking. This eliminates trailing, bloated stars, and cloud-affected frames without manual inspection.
body.p_25: Automatic background modeling using mesh interpolation. The extracted background is subtracted from each frame, removing gradient artifacts from light pollution, moon glow, and vignetting.
body.p_26: Three stacking algorithms: median for maximum outlier rejection, mean for best signal-to-noise, and sigma-clipped mean for a balance of both. Stacks update in real time as new frames arrive, producing a progressively deeper image throughout the session.
body.p_27: 8-bit preview images exported as PNG, JPEG, or TIFF for quick sharing. The full 16-bit or 32-bit FITS data is preserved separately for scientific processing.
body.p_28: An observatory session is not a single action — it is a sequence of decisions, adjustments, and contingencies that unfold over hours. HYBRID automates the entire workflow.
body.p_29: Targets are queued with altitude constraints, moon avoidance windows, and priority scores. The scheduler reorders the queue in real time as conditions change — a target rising above the horizon takes precedence over one sinking behind trees.
body.p_30: When the mount approaches the meridian, HYBRID pauses imaging, verifies pier side, flips the mount, re-centers the target, and resumes — all without user intervention. The plate-solve feedback loop ensures the target is re-acquired within 2 arcseconds of its pre-flip position.
body.p_31: PHD2 integration via ASCOM. Dithering between frames reduces walking noise patterns. Guiding calibration is automated at session start, and guide star selection uses the same HFR metrics as the main imaging pipeline.
body.p_32: N-step sequences with loops, conditional logic, and sub-frame selection. A typical sequence might be: 20×300s L, 15×300s R, 15×300s G, 15×300s B, each with autofocus triggers every 10 frames and flat calibration between filter changes.
body.p_33: Weather data from OpenWeatherMap and Meteoblue is polled continuously. Wind speed, cloud cover, and humidity thresholds trigger automatic session abort. The safety monitor is independent of the main control loop — it cannot be overridden by voice commands or automation scripts.
body.p_34: The remaining eight features in the core package address system reliability, remote access, and integration with the broader home automation ecosystem.
body.p_35: A background monitor that detects application freezes and triggers automatic restart. The watchdog preserves session state across restarts — no data is lost when the UI crashes.
body.p_36: Multi-machine architecture allows remote monitoring from any device with a web browser. The laptop next to the mount handles hardware communication; a desktop across the house provides the control interface. Both stay synchronized via WebSocket state replication.
body.p_37: Real-time status display — mount position, camera state, weather, guiding error, stacking progress — served from the local machine. No installation required on the viewing device. Works on phones, tablets, and laptops.
body.p_38: Every session is recorded: target coordinates, exposure times, guiding metrics, weather conditions, and user actions. The database is fully searchable, enabling historical analysis of tracking performance across months of data.
body.p_39: Observatory state is published to an MQTT broker for home automation integration. Node-RED, Home Assistant, and custom scripts can subscribe to mount position, session status, and safety alerts.
body.p_40: Over 60 endpoints expose every subsystem. The API is documented, versioned, and supports both JSON and message-pack serialization. Custom frontends, mobile apps, and third-party tools can control the observatory without touching the HYBRID UI.
body.p_41: The final four features target advanced users who operate multi-equipment setups and need enterprise-grade data management.
body.p_42: Saved configurations for telescope, camera, mount, and filter wheel combinations. Switching between a wide-field refractor setup and a deep-sky SCT rig takes one click instead of reconfiguring every parameter manually.
body.p_43: Two weather data sources — OpenWeatherMap for general conditions and Meteoblue for astronomical seeing forecasts. Both are polled at configurable intervals, and the data feeds directly into the safety monitor and scheduler.
body.p_44: The daemon, web, and desktop components run on separate machines. The daemon handles hardware communication on the mount-side laptop. The web dashboard runs on any device. The desktop app provides the full Electron experience on a control room machine. State synchronization is automatic and lossless.
body.p_45: Full session data export in JSON format. Import previous sessions for comparison, reproduce imaging runs, or transfer configurations to a new machine. The export includes target metadata, exposure sequences, guiding logs, and weather records.
